topics.title: Pleural Fluid Interpretation
topics.html:
[!important]
Pleural fluid analysis is essential to determine the cause of an effusion. Light's criteria separate transudate (systemic cause) from exudate (pleural/local cause). Fluid appearance, biochemistry, microbiology, and cytology guide specific management.

Related: [[Pleural Effusion]], [[Empyema and pleural infection]], [[Malignant pleural effusion]], [[Parapneumonic effusion]], [[Pneumothorax]]

[!tip] FCPS/MRCP pearl: Light's criteria: exudate = fluid/serum protein >0.5, OR fluid/serum LDH >0.6, OR fluid LDH >2/3 upper limit of serum normal. Exudate = infection, malignancy, PE, autoimmune. Transudate = heart failure, cirrhosis, nephrotic.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Differentiate transudate vs exudate (Light's criteria)
  • Interpret pleural fluid appearance, pH, glucose, LDH, protein
  • Apply to specific disease states
  • Recognise complicated parapneumonic effusion and empyema
  • Choose appropriate further investigations

2. Pleural Fluid Formation & Circulation

  • Starling forces: hydrostatic pressure (capillaries) vs oncotic pressure (plasma)
  • Production: parietal pleura (capillary filtration)
  • Absorption: visceral pleura (lymphatics)
  • Imbalance → effusion (↑production or ↓absorption)

3. Transudate vs Exudate

Light's criteria

Exudate if any ONE of:

  1. Pleural fluid protein / serum protein >0.5
  2. Pleural fluid LDH / serum LDH >0.6
  3. Pleural fluid LDH >2/3 upper limit of normal serum LDH

Transudate causes

  • Heart failure (most common)
  • Hepatic cirrhosis
  • Nephrotic syndrome
  • Hypoalbuminaemia
  • Peritoneal dialysis
  • Constrictive pericarditis
  • Atelectasis

Exudate causes

  • Infection: pneumonia (parapneumonic), empyema, TB, viral
  • Malignancy: lung, breast, lymphoma, mesothelioma
  • PE (often exudative)
  • Autoimmune: RA, SLE, ABPA
  • Pancreatitis (high amylase)
  • Drugs (nitrofurantoin, methotrexate, amiodarone)
  • Post-cardiac injury syndrome (Dressler)
  • Chylothorax (triglycerides)

4. Pleural Fluid Analysis

Appearance

AppearanceSignificance
Clear/strawTransudate, early exudate
Turbid/opaqueInfection, inflammation, chylothorax
BloodyMalignancy, trauma, PE, post-cardiac injury
Frank bloodHaemothorax (Hct >50% blood)
PurulentEmpyema
White/milkyChylothorax (triglycerides), pseudochylothorax (cholesterol)
Food particlesOesophageal rupture (Boerhaave)
BlackAspergillus infection
Anchovy pasteRuptured amoebic liver abscess
BrownChronic rupture
GreenPseudomonas

Biochemistry

TestTransudateExudateSpecific
Protein<30 g/L>30 g/LLight's
LDH<200 IU/L>200 IU/LLight's
Protein ratio<0.5>0.5Light's
LDH ratio<0.6>0.6Light's
GlucoseNormal↓ (infection, RA)<3.3 mmol/L: empyema, RA, TB
pHNormal (7.60)↓<7.2: complicated parapneumonic, empyema, oesophageal rupture, RA
AmylaseNormal↑Pancreatitis, oesophageal rupture, malignancy
Triglycerides——>1.24 mmol/L (or 110 mg/dL): chylothorax
Cholesterol——>5.18 mmol/L: pseudochylothorax
Bilirubin——Fluid/serum >0.6: complicated parapneumonic
Adenosine deaminase (ADA)——>40 U/L: TB (in lymphocyte-predominant)

Cell count and differential

Cell typeSignificance
Mesothelial cells>5%: argues against TB
LymphocytesTB, malignancy, sarcoidosis, chylothorax
NeutrophilsParapneumonic, PE, pancreatitis, subphrenic abscess
EosinophilsAir/blood in pleura, drugs, asbestos, parasitic, malignancy
Mesothelial cellsExudate

pH interpretation

pHSignificance
>7.30Normal/exudate, non-complicated
7.20–7.30Borderline; consider drainage
<7.20Complicated parapneumonic effusion, empyema, oesophageal rupture, RA, TB
<6.0Oesophageal rupture (very low)

5. Specific Conditions

Complicated parapneumonic effusion / Empyema

Criteria for chest drain:

  • pH <7.2
  • Glucose <3.3 mmol/L
  • LDH >1000 IU/L
  • Frank pus (empyema)
  • Positive Gram stain/culture
  • Loculated

Malignant effusion

  • Cytology positive in ~60% (single sample); higher with repeat
  • Lymphocyte-predominant
  • ↓glucose, ↓pH
  • Often massive (>1500 mL on CXR)

Tuberculous pleural effusion

  • Lymphocyte-predominant
  • ↑ADA (>40 U/L; high sensitivity/specificity)
  • ↑IFN-γ
  • Mesothelial cells <5% (argues against)
  • Culture positive in <20%

Chylothorax

  • Triglycerides >1.24 mmol/L (>110 mg/dL) OR chylomicrons
  • Causes: trauma, surgery, malignancy (lymphoma), congenital
  • Milky white appearance
  • Treatment: drainage, low-fat diet, MCT, surgery (thoracic duct ligation)

Pseudochylothorax

  • Cholesterol crystals, ↑cholesterol
  • Chronic (>5 years) effusion (TB, RA)
  • Treatment: drainage, decortication

Empyema

  • Frank pus, pH <7.0, glucose very low, LDH very high
  • 3 stages: exudative → fibrinopurulent → organising
  • Treatment: antibiotics + chest drain ± fibrinolytics ± VATS decortication

Haemothorax

  • Fluid Hct >50% of blood Hct
  • Trauma, iatrogenic, malignancy, PE
  • Treatment: chest drain, VATS if large/ongoing

6. Diagnostic Approach

Initial

  1. History + exam (cause)
  2. CXR (laterality, size, meniscus)
  3. Pleural US (guides tap, septations)
  4. Diagnostic thoracentesis (Light's criteria + cell count + culture + cytology + pH + glucose + LDH + protein)

Further (if exudate and not diagnosed)

  1. CT chest (malignancy, PE, infection)
  2. Pleural biopsy (image-guided or medical thoracoscopy) — for TB, malignancy
  3. Thoracoscopy (VATS or medical) — best for malignancy
  4. Indwelling pleural catheter — for recurrent malignant

7. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

DomainKey points
Light's criteriaExudate = protein ratio >0.5, LDH ratio >0.6, or LDH >2/3 ULN
TransudateHeart failure, cirrhosis, nephrotic
ExudateInfection, malignancy, PE, autoimmune
pH <7.2Complicated parapneumonic, empyema, RA, TB, oesophageal rupture
Glucose <3.3Empyema, RA, TB
ADA >40TB
Triglycerides >1.24Chylothorax
LymphocytesTB, malignancy, sarcoid
NeutrophilsParapneumonic, PE, pancreatitis
Frank pusEmpyema (drain)
Frank bloodHaemothorax

8. MCQs (10)

  1. Light's criteria: exudate if:

A. Protein ratio >0.3

B. Any one of: protein ratio >0.5, LDH ratio >0.6, fluid LDH >2/3 ULN

C. LDH <200

D. Glucose >5

E. pH >7.4

Answer: B — Any 1 of 3 criteria.

  1. Most common cause of transudative effusion:

A. Cirrhosis

B. Heart failure

C. Malignancy

D. PE

E. TB

Answer: B — Heart failure.

  1. ADA >40 U/L in lymphocyte-predominant pleural fluid suggests:

A. Malignancy

B. TB

C. Empyema

D. RA

E. PE

Answer: B — TB.

  1. Pleural fluid pH <7.2 suggests:

A. Heart failure

B. Empyema, complicated parapneumonic, oesophageal rupture, RA

C. TB

D. Malignancy

E. Viral

Answer: B — Complicated effusions.

  1. Chylothorax is diagnosed by:

A. pH <7.0

B. Triglycerides >1.24 mmol/L

C. ↑LDH

D. ↑Protein

E. ↑WBC

Answer: B — Triglycerides.

  1. Pleural fluid in RA typically shows:

A. ↓Glucose + ↓pH + high LDH

B. ↑Glucose

C. ↑Triglycerides

D. Blood

E. None

Answer: A — RA: ↓glucose, ↓pH.

  1. Pleural fluid in TB is typically:

A. Neutrophil-predominant

B. Lymphocyte-predominant with ↑ADA

C. Eosinophil-predominant

D. Mesothelial

E. Acellular

Answer: B — TB: lymphocyte + ↑ADA.

  1. Frank pus in pleural cavity = empyema; immediate treatment:

A. Antibiotics only

B. Antibiotics + chest drain (large-bore)

C. Observation

D. Discharge

E. Steroids

Answer: B — Drain + antibiotics.

  1. Pseudochylothorax is associated with:

A. Trauma

B. Chronic (>5 years) effusion (TB, RA)

C. Acute

D. Infection

E. PE

Answer: B — Chronic effusion.

  1. Light's criteria all 3 negative. Pleural fluid is:

A. Exudate

B. Transudate

C. Mixed

D. Indeterminate

E. None

Answer: B — All 3 negative = transudate.

9. SBA Questions (10)

  1. A 70-year-old with CHF has bilateral effusions. Pleural tap: protein ratio 0.3, LDH ratio 0.4. Type:

A. Exudate

B. Transudate (CHF)

C. Empyema

D. Malignancy

E. TB

Answer: B — Transudate (CHF).

  1. Pleural fluid is turbid white. Diagnosis:

A. Empyema

B. Chylothorax (or pseudochylothorax)

C. RA

D. TB

E. Malignancy

Answer: B — Milky = chyle or pseudochyle.

  1. Complicated parapneumonic effusion diagnosis:

A. Positive culture

B. pH <7.2 OR glucose <3.3 OR LDH >1000 OR frank pus

C. Lymphocytes

D. ↑WBC

E. Any effusion

Answer: B — pH/glucose/LDH criteria.

  1. Pleural fluid amylase ↑ in:

A. TB

B. Pancreatitis, oesophageal rupture, malignancy

C. CHF

D. PE

E. RA

Answer: B — Pancreas, oesophagus, malignancy.

  1. A 60-year-old has pleural fluid pH 6.8, glucose 2.0. Most likely:

A. Heart failure

B. Empyema or oesophageal rupture

C. TB

D. Malignancy

E. Viral

Answer: B — Very low pH + glucose.

  1. Best next step if Light's criteria positive (exudate) but cause unclear:

A. Discharge

B. CT chest +/− pleural biopsy

C. Antibiotics

D. Steroids

E. Observation

Answer: B — Further imaging + biopsy.

  1. Most sensitive test for TB pleural effusion:

A. ADA

B. Pleural biopsy (culture/histology)

C. Sputum

D. CXR

E. Pleural fluid cytology

Answer: B — Pleural biopsy is most sensitive.

  1. The most common cause of malignant pleural effusion:

A. Breast

B. Lung cancer

C. Lymphoma

D. Mesothelioma

E. Ovarian

Answer: B — Lung most common.

  1. Bilateral effusions with normal-sized heart on CXR. Cause:

A. Heart failure

B. Liver cirrhosis or nephrotic syndrome

C. Malignancy

D. TB

E. PE

Answer: B — Bilateral effusions with normal heart suggest non-cardiac cause.

  1. Empyema at 5 days (fibrinopurulent stage). Best treatment:

A. Antibiotics only

B. Antibiotics + chest drain +/− intrapleural fibrinolytics (e.g. urokinase)

C. Surgery

D. Discharge

E. Wait

Answer: B — Drain + fibrinolytics.

10. Flashcards

  • Q: Light's criteria for exudate?

A: Any 1 of: protein ratio >0.5, LDH ratio >0.6, LDH >2/3 ULN.

  • Q: Most common transudate cause?

A: Heart failure.

  • Q: ADA >40?

A: TB.

  • Q: Pleural fluid pH <7.2?

A: Complicated parapneumonic, empyema, oesophageal rupture, RA, TB.

  • Q: Triglycerides >1.24?

A: Chylothorax.

  • Q: Frank pus?

A: Empyema.

  • Q: Milky fluid?

A: Chylothorax or pseudochylothorax.

  • Q: Most common exudate?

A: Infection, malignancy.

  • Q: Glucose <3.3 in pleural fluid?

A: Empyema, RA, TB.

  • Q: Pleural biopsy most sensitive for?

A: TB.

11. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. B 2. B 3. B 4. B 5. B 6. A 7. B 8. B 9. B 10. B

SBAs

  1. B 2. B 3. B 4. B 5. B 6. B 7. B 8. B 9. B 10. B

12. Summary

Pleural fluid interpretation uses Light's criteria to differentiate transudate (CHF, cirrhosis, nephrotic) from exudate (infection, malignancy, PE, autoimmune). pH <7.2, glucose <3.3, LDH >1000 indicate complicated parapneumonic effusion/empyema. ADA >40 in lymphocyte-rich fluid = TB. Triglycerides >1.24 = chylothorax. Frank pus = empyema. Further workup with CT + pleural biopsy if exudate unclear.

topics.title: Ventilation Perfusion Mismatch And Shunt
topics.html:
[!important]
V/Q mismatch is the most common cause of hypoxaemia in lung disease. Shunt (perfusion without ventilation) is the most refractory to O₂ therapy. Understanding the V/Q spectrum is essential for interpreting ABG, A-a gradient, and response to O₂.

Related: [[ABG Interpretation]], [[Oxygen Therapy and NIV]], [[Respiratory Failure]], [[Spirometry Interpretation]]

[!tip] FCPS/MRCP pearl: V/Q mismatch responds to supplemental O₂; true shunt does not (or responds poorly). V/Q = 1 = perfect match; V/Q = 0 = pure shunt; V/Q = ∞ = dead space.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Define V/Q ratio and its spectrum
  • Distinguish V/Q mismatch, shunt, and dead space
  • Calculate A-a gradient and identify causes
  • Predict response to O₂ therapy
  • Apply to common disease states

2. Definition

V/Q ratio = ratio of alveolar ventilation (V) to pulmonary capillary blood flow (Q).

V/Q valueMeaningExample
0Pure shunt (perfusion without ventilation)Pneumonia, atelectasis, ARDS, complete airway obstruction
0 < V/Q < 1Low V/Q (relative shunt)Early pneumonia, mild airway obstruction
1Perfect matchNormal lung units
1 < V/Q < ∞High V/Q (wasted ventilation / relative dead space)PE, early COPD
∞Pure dead space (ventilation without perfusion)PE (complete obstruction)

3. Pathophysiology

Normal V/Q distribution

  • Apex: V/Q ≈ 3.3 (high V, low Q → relative dead space)
  • Base: V/Q ≈ 0.6 (low V, high Q → relative shunt)
  • Lung is never perfectly matched — normal A-a gradient is 5–15 mmHg (1–2 kPa)

Hypoxaemia mechanisms

  1. Hypoventilation (low V/Q = 0) → ↑PaCO₂ + ↓PaO₂
  2. V/Q mismatch (low V/Q) → ↓PaO₂, variable PaCO₂
  3. Shunt (V/Q = 0) → ↓PaO₂ refractory to O₂
  4. Diffusion limitation → ↓PaO₂ (esp. exercise)
  5. Low FiO₂ (high altitude) → ↓PaO₂

Shunt vs V/Q mismatch

FeatureV/Q mismatchTrue shunt
CauseUnder-ventilated, perfused alveoliNo ventilation, perfused
PaO₂ response to 100% O₂Improves (PaO₂ >500 mmHg)No/minimal improvement
A-a gradient↑↑↑↑
PaCO₂VariableUsually normal/low (compensatory)
ExamplesAsthma, COPD, mild pneumoniaARDS, lobar pneumonia, severe atelectasis, R→L cardiac shunt

4. Clinical Applications

V/Q mismatch states

  • COPD/asthma: perfusion of poorly ventilated areas (low V/Q)
  • Pneumonia (early): partial consolidation
  • Pulmonary oedema: fluid impairs gas exchange
  • ILD: thickened alveolar-capillary membrane

True shunt states

  • Pneumonia (consolidation)
  • ARDS (alveolar collapse + fluid)
  • Atelectasis
  • Pulmonary AVM (anatomical shunt)
  • R→L intracardiac shunt (e.g. PFO, ASD with Eisenmenger)
  • Hepatopulmonary syndrome

Dead space (high V/Q)

  • PE (ventilated, no perfusion)
  • Severe COPD (alveolar destruction)
  • Low cardiac output states
  • PE contributes to ↑PaCO₂ and ↑A-a gradient

5. Diagnosis

A-a gradient

A-a gradient = PAO₂ − PaO₂ (normal 5–15 mmHg / 1–2 kPa on room air; increases with age)

  • PAO₂ = FiO₂ × (Patm − PH₂O) − PaCO₂/R = 0.21 × (760 − 47) − PaCO₂/0.8 ≈ 150 − PaCO₂/0.8 (room air)
  • Causes of ↑A-a gradient: V/Q mismatch, shunt, diffusion limitation
  • Normal A-a gradient + ↓PaO₂ + ↑PaCO₂ = hypoventilation (e.g. opioid, NM weakness)

Shunt fraction calculation

  • Iso-shunt diagram (classic): PaO₂ vs FiO₂ for given shunt fractions
  • 50% shunt = PaO₂ <60 mmHg on 100% O₂
  • Clinical: 100% O₂ for 15–20 min; if PaO₂ doesn't rise above 500 mmHg, significant shunt

V/Q scan

  • Clinical use: suspected PE
  • V = ventilation, Q = perfusion
  • Matched defect = normal; mismatched (V normal, Q absent) = PE
  • Indeterminate: intermediate probability

6. Management

V/Q mismatch

  • Treat underlying cause (bronchodilators, antibiotics, diuretics)
  • Supplemental O₂ to correct hypoxaemia
  • Usually responds well

True shunt

  • Treat underlying cause (consolidation, atelectasis)
  • O₂ often ineffective at correcting
  • PEEP recruits collapsed alveoli (ARDS, atelectasis)
  • Positioning (prone, lateral decubitus to good lung down)
  • ECMO in severe refractory cases

7. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

DomainKey points
Normal V/Q1.0 (perfect match)
Lung apex V/Q~3.3 (high V, low Q)
Lung base V/Q~0.6 (low V, high Q)
V/Q mismatchMost common cause of hypoxaemia in lung disease
ShuntRefractory to O₂ (PaO₂ <500 on 100% O₂)
V/Q response to O₂Yes, improves
A-a gradient↑ in V/Q mismatch, shunt, diffusion limitation; normal in hypoventilation
Hypoventilation pattern↓PaO₂ + ↑PaCO₂ + normal A-a gradient
PEHigh V/Q (dead space), ↑A-a gradient, ↓PaCO₂ (hypervent)
ARDSSevere shunt; prone positioning recruits alveoli

8. MCQs (10)

  1. Normal V/Q ratio in healthy lung is:

A. 0.5

B. 1.0

C. 2.0

D. 0.1

E. 5.0

Answer: B — V/Q = 1 is the ideal.

  1. A patient with hypoxaemia is given 100% O₂ for 20 min. PaO₂ rises from 50 to 380 mmHg. Mechanism of hypoxaemia:

A. True shunt

B. V/Q mismatch

C. Hypoventilation

D. Diffusion limitation

E. Low FiO₂

Answer: B — Significant improvement with O₂ = V/Q mismatch.

  1. A patient with hypoxaemia is given 100% O₂ for 20 min. PaO₂ rises from 50 to 55 mmHg. Mechanism:

A. V/Q mismatch

B. True shunt

C. Hypoventilation

D. Anaemia

E. Altitude

Answer: B — Minimal response to O₂ = true shunt.

  1. A-a gradient is normal in:

A. Pneumonia

B. PE

C. Hypoventilation (e.g. opioid overdose)

D. ARDS

E. ILD

Answer: C — Hypoventilation = normal A-a gradient (low V, normal Q).

  1. The most common cause of hypoxaemia in lung disease is:

A. Shunt

B. V/Q mismatch

C. Hypoventilation

D. Diffusion

E. Low FiO₂

Answer: B — V/Q mismatch most common.

  1. ARDS hypoxaemia is due to:

A. V/Q mismatch

B. True shunt (collapsed/fluid-filled alveoli)

C. Hypoventilation

D. Anaemia

E. Altitude

Answer: B — ARDS = severe shunt.

  1. PE causes hypoxaemia via:

A. Shunt

B. Increased dead space (V/Q ↑)

C. V/Q mismatch

D. Anaemia

E. Altitude

Answer: B — Dead space (high V/Q).

  1. A 60-year-old with COPD and PaO₂ 55 mmHg, PaCO₂ 50 mmHg, A-a gradient 30. Mechanism:

A. Pure hypoventilation

B. V/Q mismatch

C. Shunt

D. Anaemia

E. Altitude

Answer: B — ↑A-a gradient = V/Q mismatch (or shunt).

  1. The PaO₂ on 100% O₂ in a 50% shunt is:

A. >500 mmHg

B. <60 mmHg

C. Normal

D. 200 mmHg

E. 100 mmHg

Answer: B — 50% shunt has refractory hypoxaemia.

  1. A patient with hypoxaemia has normal A-a gradient and ↑PaCO₂. Diagnosis:

A. V/Q mismatch

B. Shunt

C. Pure hypoventilation (e.g. opioid overdose)

D. PE

E. Anaemia

Answer: C — Normal A-a + ↑PaCO₂ = hypoventilation.

9. SBA Questions (10)

  1. A patient has hypoxaemia, ↑A-a gradient, ↑PaCO₂. Best first treatment:

A. Intubation

B. Supplemental O₂ + treat underlying cause

C. Hyperventilation

D. Steroids

E. Antibiotics

Answer: B — O₂ + treat cause.

  1. PE ABG typically shows:

A. ↑PaCO₂

B. ↓PaO₂ + ↓PaCO₂ (hyperventilation) + ↑A-a gradient

C. Normal

D. ↑PaO₂

E. ↑pH

Answer: B — Hypoxaemia + respiratory alkalosis (compensatory hyperventilation).

  1. A patient with severe ARDS has PaO₂ 60 mmHg on 100% O₂. P/F ratio:

A. 60

B. 60 (severe ARDS)

C. 300

D. 200

E. 100

Answer: B — P/F 60 = severe ARDS by Berlin definition.

  1. First-line treatment for shunt in ARDS:

A. Bronchodilators

B. PEEP to recruit collapsed alveoli

C. Steroids

D. Antibiotics

E. Diuretics

Answer: B — PEEP recruits.

  1. A patient with hypoxaemia has ↓PaO₂ + normal PaCO₂ + ↑A-a gradient. Mechanism:

A. Hypoventilation

B. V/Q mismatch (most likely) or shunt

C. Altitude

D. Anaemia

E. Anxiety

Answer: B — ↑A-a gradient + ↓PaCO₂ = V/Q mismatch or shunt.

  1. The V/Q scan interpretation in PE: matched defect means:

A. PE

B. Normal or other cause

C. Pneumonia

D. PE confirmed

E. COPD

Answer: B — Matched = normal.

  1. Hepatopulmonary syndrome is associated with:

A. Hypoxaemia + intrapulmonary shunts + liver disease

B. Hypercapnia

C. Normal ABG

D. Pleural effusion

E. No symptoms

Answer: A — HPS = liver disease + intrapulmonary vascular dilatations + hypoxaemia.

  1. PFO with R→L shunt causes:

A. ↑PaO₂

B. ↓PaO₂ refractory to O₂, platypnoea-orthodeoxia

C. Normal

D. ↑PaCO₂

E. No symptoms

Answer: B — Anatomical shunt.

  1. Dead space ventilation in PE leads to:

A. ↓PaCO₂

B. Initial hyperventilation → ↓PaCO₂ (if V/Q still adequate)

C. ↑PaCO₂

D. No effect

E. Hyperoxia

Answer: B — Hyperventilation compensates.

  1. Treatment of hypoxaemia due to V/Q mismatch:

A. ECMO

B. Supplemental O₂ + treat underlying cause

C. Tracheostomy

D. Steroids only

E. Surgery

Answer: B — O₂ + treat.

10. Flashcards

  • Q: Normal V/Q ratio?

A: 1.0.

  • Q: V/Q at lung apex?

A: ~3.3 (high V, low Q).

  • Q: V/Q at lung base?

A: ~0.6 (low V, high Q).

  • Q: What does shunt not respond to?

A: 100% O₂ (refractory hypoxaemia).

  • Q: Normal A-a gradient?

A: 5–15 mmHg (1–2 kPa) on room air.

  • Q: Cause of ↑A-a gradient?

A: V/Q mismatch, shunt, diffusion limitation.

  • Q: Cause of normal A-a gradient + ↓PaO₂?

A: Hypoventilation.

  • Q: V/Q ratio in PE?

A: High (dead space).

  • Q: Most common cause of hypoxaemia in lung disease?

A: V/Q mismatch.

  • Q: Treatment of shunt in ARDS?

A: PEEP (recruit alveoli).

11. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. B 2. B 3. B 4. C 5. B 6. B 7. B 8. B 9. B 10. C

SBAs

  1. B 2. B 3. B 4. B 5. B 6. B 7. A 8. B 9. B 10. B

12. Summary

V/Q mismatch and shunt are the two main mechanisms of hypoxaemia in lung disease. V/Q mismatch is the most common and responds to O₂; shunt is refractory to O₂ and requires PEEP/recruitment. Normal A-a gradient suggests hypoventilation; ↑A-a gradient suggests V/Q mismatch, shunt, or diffusion limitation.

topics.title: Work Of Breathing And Respiratory Muscles
topics.html:
[!important]
Work of breathing (WOB) is the energy expended by respiratory muscles to overcome elastic and resistive loads. It is markedly increased in obstructive (asthma, COPD) and restrictive (ILD, chest wall) disease, and can lead to respiratory muscle fatigue and failure if sustained.

Related: [[Respiratory Failure]], [[Neuromuscular respiratory weakness]], [[Chest wall restriction and kyphoscoliosis]], [[ABG Interpretation]]

[!tip] FCPS/MRCP pearl: WOB = 0.5 J/L normally; ↑3–10× in disease. Respiratory muscle fatigue is a key reason for ventilatory failure in NM disorders, severe asthma, and COPD. Accessory muscle use, paradoxical breathing, and ↑RR signal impending fatigue.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Define work of breathing and its components
  • Identify respiratory muscles (primary and accessory)
  • Recognise clinical signs of increased WOB
  • Understand the work equation (Campbell diagram)
  • Apply to disease states
  • Manage respiratory muscle fatigue

2. Definition

Work of breathing (WOB) = energy required to move the lungs and chest wall during breathing. Normal quiet breathing: 0.5 J/L (~3–5% of total body O₂ consumption).

Components of WOB

ComponentDescriptionContributors
Elastic workOvercome elastic recoil (lung + chest wall)Surface tension, elastin, collagen
Resistive workOvercome airway and tissue resistanceAirway diameter, flow rate, viscosity
Inertive workOvercome inertia of tissues/gasUsually negligible

Work equation

W = ∫P × dV (area of pressure-volume loop)

  • P = pressure generated by respiratory muscles
  • V = volume moved
  • Work done = area inside the pressure-volume loop (Campbell diagram)

O₂ cost of breathing

  • Normal: 3–5% of total VO₂ (rest)
  • Mild disease: 10–15%
  • Severe disease (e.g. ARDS): 25–30% or more

3. Respiratory Muscles

Primary inspiratory muscles

MuscleInnervationAction
DiaphragmPhrenic nerve (C3–C5)Principal muscle of inspiration; ↓diaphragm contracts → inspiration
External intercostalsIntercostal nervesLift ribs, expand thoracic cage
ScalenesCervical spinal nervesStabilise/elevate upper ribs (active in normal inspiration)

Accessory inspiratory muscles

  • Sternocleidomastoid (CN XI) — lifts sternum
  • Pectoralis major/minor — arm fixed, pulls sternum
  • Trapezius, serratus anterior — shoulder fixation

Expiratory muscles (active in disease, not normal quiet breathing)

  • Internal intercostals
  • Abdominal muscles (rectus, obliques, transversus)

Other muscles

  • Muscles of upper airway (genioglossus, etc.) — maintain airway patency

4. Pathophysiology

WOB in disease

DiseaseMechanismWOB
Asthma/COPD↑Airway resistance + dynamic hyperinflation (flattens diaphragm)↑↑
ILD↓Compliance (elastic work ↑)↑
Pneumonia/consolidation↓Compliance + shunt↑
Pulmonary oedema↓Compliance + ↑fluid↑↑
Chest wall disease (kyphoscoliosis, obesity)↓Chest wall compliance↑
NM weakness (GBS, MG)↓Muscle strength↑ to maintain ventilation
Pleural effusion/PTX↓Lung expansion↑

Pressure-volume relationships

  • Compliance (C) = ΔV/ΔP
  • ↓Compliance → more work to expand
  • ↑Compliance (e.g. emphysema) → elastic recoil lost, more work to exhale

Muscle fatigue

  • WOB > critical threshold → muscle fatigue → respiratory failure
  • Diaphragm fatigue when:
  • Pdi (transdiaphragmatic pressure) <40% of maximum
  • Sustained for >45 min
  • MVV (max voluntary ventilation) falls with fatigue

5. Clinical Signs of Increased WOB

Inspection

  • Tachypnoea (most sensitive)
  • Use of accessory muscles (SCM, scalenes)
  • Intercostal/subcostal recession
  • Tracheal tug (downward pull on inspiration)
  • Paradoxical breathing (abdominal paradox — sign of diaphragmatic fatigue)
  • Nasal flaring
  • Pursed-lip breathing (COPD — auto-PEEP)
  • Tripoding (leaning forward)

Palpation/percussion

  • ↓Chest expansion
  • Hyperresonance (obstructive)

Auscultation

  • Wheeze (obstructive)
  • Crackles (ILD, oedema)
  • Diminished breath sounds (NM, severe obstruction)

6. Diagnosis

Spirometry

  • FEV₁, FVC, MVV — indirect measures of muscle strength
  • MVV <60% predicted suggests muscle weakness
  • Vital capacity <1 L or <15 mL/kg = severe muscle weakness

Maximum inspiratory/expiratory pressures

  • MIP (Pi_max) = max inspiratory pressure at mouth (normal: >−80 cmH₂O)
  • MEP (Pe_max) = max expiratory pressure
  • ↓MIP suggests respiratory muscle weakness (GBS, MG, ALS, muscular dystrophy)

Electromyography (EMG) of diaphragm

  • Assess phrenic nerve function
  • Useful in NM disorders

Ultrasonography

  • Diaphragm thickness and movement
  • ↑Thickness fraction with inspiration = normal (>30%)

7. Management

Reduce WOB

  • Treat underlying disease (bronchodilators, steroids, diuretics, antibiotics)
  • Position: sitting up, leaning forward (improves diaphragm mechanics)
  • O₂ therapy (reduces drive to hyperventilate)
  • CPAP/BiPAP (offloads respiratory muscles)
  • Treat pain (reduces splinting)

Support respiratory muscles

  • NIV (CPAP for OSA, BiPAP for hypercapnic failure)
  • Mechanical ventilation (invasive if needed)
  • Physiotherapy (clearance, breathing exercises)
  • Nutritional support (avoid overfeeding, maintain muscle mass)

Treat underlying cause

  • NM weakness: treat GBS (IVIG/plasma exchange), MG (pyridostigmine, immunosuppression)
  • Chest wall: braces, weight loss (obesity), surgical correction (kyphoscoliosis)
  • COPD: bronchodilators, oxygen, pulmonary rehab
  • Asthma: bronchodilators, steroids

8. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

DomainKey points
Normal WOB0.5 J/L
ComponentsElastic + resistive + (inertive)
O₂ cost3–5% normal, ↑ in disease
DiaphragmC3–C5 (phrenic), principal inspiratory
Accessory musclesSCM, scalenes, pectorals (active in disease)
WOB ↑inAsthma, COPD, ILD, NM weakness, obesity, kyphoscoliosis
Signs of ↑WOBTachypnoea, accessory muscle use, recession, paradoxical breathing
Abdominal paradoxSign of diaphragmatic fatigue
MVVFalls with muscle weakness; <60% = significant
MIPMax inspiratory pressure (normal >−80 cmH₂O)
Pursed-lip breathingAuto-PEEP in COPD
PositionSitting/leaning forward reduces WOB

9. MCQs (10)

  1. Normal work of breathing is approximately:

A. 0.05 J/L

B. 0.5 J/L

C. 5 J/L

D. 50 J/L

E. 500 J/L

Answer: B — 0.5 J/L normally.

  1. The principal muscle of inspiration is:

A. External intercostals

B. Diaphragm

C. SCM

D. Pectoralis

E. Scalenes

Answer: B — Diaphragm.

  1. The diaphragm is innervated by:

A. Vagus

B. Phrenic nerve (C3–C5)

C. Intercostal nerves

D. Sympathetic trunk

E. Phrenic nerve (C7–T1)

Answer: B — C3–C5.

  1. O₂ cost of breathing in normal quiet breathing is:

A. <1%

B. 3–5%

C. 15–20%

D. 30–40%

E. >50%

Answer: B — 3–5%.

  1. A sign of diaphragmatic fatigue is:

A. Tachypnoea

B. Abdominal paradox

C. Wheeze

D. Cyanosis

E. Cough

Answer: B — Diaphragm fatigue → abdominal paradox.

  1. Pursed-lip breathing is a sign of:

A. Asthma

B. COPD with auto-PEEP

C. Pneumonia

D. PE

E. Heart failure

Answer: B — Auto-PEEP in COPD.

  1. Normal maximum inspiratory pressure (MIP) is approximately:

A. −20 cmH₂O

B. −80 cmH₂O

C. −150 cmH₂O

D. +50 cmH₂O

E. +200 cmH₂O

Answer: B — >−80 cmH₂O.

  1. Position that reduces WOB in COPD/CHF:

A. Supine

B. Sitting up, leaning forward

C. Prone

D. Lateral decubitus

E. Trendelenburg

Answer: B — Sits up to optimise diaphragm.

  1. WOB is increased in all of the following EXCEPT:

A. Asthma

B. Normal quiet breathing

C. ILD

D. NM weakness

E. Obesity

Answer: B — Normal has low WOB.

  1. MVV <60% predicted suggests:

A. Healthy

B. Significant respiratory muscle weakness

C. Asthma only

D. Mild disease

E. Normal aging

Answer: B — Muscle weakness.

10. SBA Questions (10)

  1. A patient with GBS has progressive dyspnoea, MVV 50%, MIP −40 cmH₂O. Action:

A. Discharge

B. Admit to HDU/ITU, consider NIV/intubation

C. Antibiotics only

D. Steroids

E. Physiotherapy only

Answer: B — NM weakness + respiratory failure risk.

  1. A 65-year-old with severe COPD has MVV 40%. Indication for:

A. NIV support during exacerbation

B. Steroids only

C. Antibiotics

D. Discharge

E. No support

Answer: A — NIV for severe COPD.

  1. O₂ cost of breathing in ARDS:

A. 5%

B. 10%

C. 25–30%

D. 50%

E. 90%

Answer: C — ↑ in ARDS.

  1. A patient with NM weakness has VC 800 mL. Next step:

A. Discharge

B. Admit, monitor VC, consider NIV

C. Antibiotics

D. Steroids

E. Wait

Answer: B — VC <1 L = severe weakness.

  1. Treatment of respiratory muscle fatigue in acute asthma:

A. Antibiotics

B. Bronchodilators + steroids + MgSO₄ (relieve load)

C. Steroids alone

D. Theophylline

E. Rest only

Answer: B — Relieve airway obstruction.

  1. A patient with diaphragmatic paralysis. Best test:

A. CXR

B. Diaphragmatic ultrasound + fluoroscopy sniff test

C. Spirometry

D. CT

E. ABG

Answer: B — Direct visualisation.

  1. A COPD patient uses accessory muscles. Significance:

A. Normal

B. Increased WOB, impending fatigue

C. Mild disease

D. Anxiety

E. Cardiac cause

Answer: B — Impending fatigue.

  1. Bilateral diaphragm paralysis causes:

A. Mild dyspnoea

B. Severe orthopnoea + hypercapnic respiratory failure

C. Asymptomatic

D. Cough only

E. Wheeze

Answer: B — Severe orthopnoea (diaphragm needed for supine).

  1. Treatment of accessory muscle use in acute asthma:

A. Rest

B. Bronchodilators, steroids, O₂ (relieve load)

C. Antibiotics

D. Surgery

E. Theophylline

Answer: B — Relieve load.

  1. Pulse oximetry target in NM weakness (acute):

A. 100%

B. 94–98% (or 88–92% if hypercapnic risk)

C. 80–85%

D. No target

E. >95% always

Answer: B — Standard target.

11. Flashcards

  • Q: Normal WOB?

A: 0.5 J/L.

  • Q: Phrenic nerve origin?

A: C3, C4, C5.

  • Q: O₂ cost of normal breathing?

A: 3–5% of total VO₂.

  • Q: Sign of diaphragmatic fatigue?

A: Abdominal paradox (inward abdominal movement on inspiration).

  • Q: Pursed-lip breathing purpose?

A: Maintains airway patency in COPD with auto-PEEP.

  • Q: Normal MIP?

A: >−80 cmH₂O.

  • Q: MVV <60% suggests?

A: Significant respiratory muscle weakness.

  • Q: Best position to reduce WOB?

A: Sitting up, leaning forward (tripoding).

  • Q: Inspiratory muscles?

A: Diaphragm (primary), external intercostals, scalenes (accessory: SCM, pectorals).

  • Q: Expiratory muscles?

A: Internal intercostals, abdominal muscles.

12. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. B 2. B 3. B 4. B 5. B 6. B 7. B 8. B 9. B 10. B

SBAs

  1. B 2. A 3. C 4. B 5. B 6. B 7. B 8. B 9. B 10. B

13. Summary

WOB = energy for breathing. Normal: 0.5 J/L. Components: elastic + resistive. Diaphragm (C3–C5) is principal. Increased in asthma, COPD, ILD, NM weakness, obesity. Clinical signs: tachypnoea, accessory muscle use, abdominal paradox. Diagnosis: spirometry, MIP, diaphragm ultrasound. Management: treat cause, position, NIV, intubation if fatigue.

topics.title: Abg Interpretation
topics.html:
Related: [[Acid-Base and Electrolyte Emergencies]], [[Respiratory Failure]], [[Oxygen Therapy and NIV]]

[!important]
Systematic ABG interpretation = essential skill for acute medicine. Stepwise approach: pH → PaCO₂ → HCO₃⁻ → compensation → anion gap → delta-delta. Key FCPS/MRCP: Henderson-Hasselbalch vs Stewart, compensation formulas (Winter's), albumin-corrected AG, delta-delta for mixed disorders, oxygenation assessment (A-a gradient).

1. Learning Objectives

  • Apply stepwise ABG interpretation algorithm
  • Identify primary acid-base disorders and appropriate compensation
  • Calculate anion gap, albumin-corrected AG, delta-delta
  • Assess oxygenation (A-a gradient, P/F ratio)
  • Recognise mixed acid-base disorders

2. Normal Values (Adult, Sea Level)

ParameterRange
pH7.35 – 7.45
PaCO₂4.7 – 6.0 kPa (35 – 45 mmHg)
PaO₂11 – 13 kPa (80 – 100 mmHg)
HCO₃⁻22 – 26 mmol/L
Base Excess-2 to +2 mmol/L
Lactate<2 mmol/L
Anion Gap8 – 12 mmol/L (albumin-corrected)

3. Stepwise ABG Interpretation (Traditional Approach)

Step 1: pH → Acidaemia or Alkalaemia?

  • pH <7.35 → Acidaemia
  • pH >7.45 → Alkalaemia
  • pH 7.35-7.45 → Normal (but may be compensated mixed disorder)

Step 2: PaCO₂ → Respiratory Component

PaCO₂Interpretation
↑ (>6.0 kPa)Respiratory acidosis (or compensation for metabolic alkalosis)
↓ (<4.7 kPa)Respiratory alkalosis (or compensation for metabolic acidosis)
NormalNo primary respiratory disorder

Step 3: HCO₃⁻ → Metabolic Component

HCO₃⁻Interpretation
↓ (<22 mmol/L)Metabolic acidosis (or compensation for respiratory alkalosis)
↑ (>26 mmol/L)Metabolic alkalosis (or compensation for respiratory acidosis)
NormalNo primary metabolic disorder

Step 4: Match Primary Disorder with pH

Primary DisorderpH Direction
Respiratory acidosis↓ (acidaemia)
Respiratory alkalosis↑ (alkalaemia)
Metabolic acidosis↓ (acidaemia)
Metabolic alkalosis↑ (alkalaemia)

Step 5: Assess Compensation (Appropriate vs Inadequate vs Mixed)

Respiratory Acidosis Compensation

TypeExpected HCO₃⁻ Change
AcuteHCO₃⁻ ↑ 1 mmol/L per 10 mmHg (1.3 kPa) PaCO₂ rise
ChronicHCO₃⁻ ↑ 4 mmol/L per 10 mmHg (1.3 kPa) PaCO₂ rise

Respiratory Alkalosis Compensation

TypeExpected HCO₃⁻ Change
AcuteHCO₃⁻ ↓ 2 mmol/L per 10 mmHg (1.3 kPa) PaCO₂ fall
ChronicHCO₃⁻ ↓ 5 mmol/L per 10 mmHg (1.3 kPa) PaCO₂ fall

Metabolic Acidosis Compensation (Winter's Formula)

Expected PaCO₂ = 1.5 × HCO₃⁻ + 8 ± 2 mmHg

Metabolic Alkalosis Compensation

Expected PaCO₂ = 0.7 × HCO₃⁻ + 20 ± 5 mmHg

Step 6: Anion Gap (If Metabolic Acidosis)

AG = Na⁺ − (Cl⁻ + HCO₃⁻)

Albumin-Corrected AG = AG + 2.5 × (40 − Albumin g/L)

MUDPILES (High AG Metabolic Acidosis):

  • Methanol, Uraemia, DKA, Paraldehyde, Iron/Isoniazid, Lactic acidosis, Ethylene glycol, Salicylate

Step 7: Delta-Delta (ΔΔ) — Unmask Mixed Metabolic Disorders

  • ΔAG = Measured AG − Normal AG (12)
  • ΔHCO₃⁻ = Normal HCO₃⁻ (24) − Measured HCO₃⁻
  • ΔΔ = ΔAG − ΔHCO₃⁻
ΔΔ ValueInterpretation
≈ 0Pure high AG metabolic acidosis
> +6 (or >0.6 mmol/L)Concomitant metabolic alkalosis
< −6 (or < −0.6 mmol/L)Concomitant normal AG metabolic acidosis

4. Oxygenation Assessment

A-a Gradient

A-a gradient = PAO₂ − PaO₂

PAO₂ = FiO₂ × (760 − 47) − PaCO₂ / 0.8 (at sea level, FiO₂ 0.21: PAO₂ = 150 − PaCO₂/0.8)

AgeNormal A-a Gradient
<30 yrs<10 mmHg
30-60 yrs10-15 mmHg
>60 yrs15-20 mmHg

Elevated A-a gradient → V/Q mismatch, shunt, diffusion limitation (NOT pure hypoventilation)

P/F Ratio (PaO₂/FiO₂)

P/F RatioSeverity (ARDS)
>300Normal / Mild
200-300Mild ARDS
100-200Moderate ARDS
<100Severe ARDS

[Truncated]

Topic Overview

1. Learning Objectives
2. Normal Values (Adult, Sea Level)
3. Stepwise ABG Interpretation (Traditional Approach)
Step 1: pH → Acidaemia or Alkalaemia?
Step 2: PaCO₂ → Respiratory Component
Step 3: HCO₃⁻ → Metabolic Component
Step 4: Match Primary Disorder with pH
Step 5: Assess Compensation (Appropriate vs Inadequate vs Mixed)
Step 6: Anion Gap (If Metabolic Acidosis)
Step 7: Delta-Delta (ΔΔ) — Unmask Mixed Metabolic Disorders
4. Oxygenation Assessment
A-a Gradient
P/F Ratio (PaO₂/FiO₂)

topics.title: Chest X Ray Approach
topics.html:
[!important]
CXR is the most commonly performed imaging in respiratory medicine. Systematic interpretation (A–B–C–D–E: Airway, Bones, Cardiac, Diaphragm, Effusion, Fields, Gastric bubble, Hilar) prevents missed findings. Always compare with previous films.

Related: [[CT chest pattern approach]], [[Pneumonia]], [[Pleural Effusion]], [[Pneumothorax]], [[Lung Cancer]]

[!tip] FCPS/MRCP pearl: Systematic approach = A-B-C-D-E-F-G-H: Airway (trachea, carina), Bones, Cardiac, Diaphragm, Effusion, Fields (lungs), Gastric bubble, Hilar. Always compare with prior films. Don't miss pneumothorax (look for lung edge, deep sulcus sign, absence of lung markings). Don't miss consolidation (silhouette sign).

1. Definition

Chest X-ray (CXR) = plain radiograph of the chest; PA (posteroanterior) preferred over AP.

2. Standard Approach

A–B–C–D–E–F–G–H

StepComponentLook for
AAirwayTrachea position, carina, narrowing
BBonesRibs, clavicles, spine, fractures, lesions
CCardiacSize (cardiothoracic ratio <0.5), shape, borders
DDiaphragmPosition, shape, free air under
EEffusion (pleural)Costophrenic angle blunting, meniscus
FFields (lungs)Consolidation, mass, collapse, vessels
HHilarSize, position, density, lymphadenopathy

Additional

  • Mediastinum: width, position, masses
  • Soft tissues: subcutaneous emphysema, breast shadows
  • Bones: ribs, clavicles, scapulae, spine
  • Foreign bodies: tubes, lines, pacemakers
  • Comparison: previous films

3. Technical Assessment

Quality

  • Rotation: medial ends of clavicles equidistant from spinous processes
  • Inspiration: ≥6 anterior ribs above diaphragm (good inspiration)
  • Penetration: vertebral bodies just visible behind heart
  • Coverage: apices to costophrenic angles

Type

  • PA (preferred; upright, full inspiration)
  • AP (supine, less accurate for heart size)
  • Lateral (localises lesions)

4. Common Patterns

Pneumothorax

  • Absent lung markings distal to visceral pleural line
  • Deep sulcus sign (deep costophrenic angle)
  • Mediastinal shift (tension PTX)
  • Tracheal deviation (away, in tension)

Pleural effusion

  • Costophrenic angle blunting (>200 mL needed for visible blunting)
  • Meniscus sign
  • Opacification of hemithorax if large
  • Loculated effusion (linear, lentiform)

Consolidation (pneumonia)

  • Homogeneous opacification
  • Air bronchograms
  • Silhouette sign (loss of normal border)
  • Lobar distribution

Lobar collapse

  • Direct signs: displacement of fissures
  • Indirect signs: ↓volume, mediastinal shift toward collapse, ↑diaphragm, rib crowding
  • Patterns: RUL (raised horizontal fissure), LUL (veil sign), RML (blurred R heart border), LLL (sail sign, ↓L diaphragm), RLL (↓R diaphragm)

Mass / nodule

  • Solitary pulmonary nodule (<3 cm)
  • Mass (>3 cm)
  • Cavitation, calcification, spiculation
  • Comparison with prior (stability vs growth)

Heart failure

  • Cardiomegaly (CT ratio >0.5 on PA)
  • Pulmonary oedema (bat-wing, Kerley B lines, effusions)
  • Upper lobe diversion (cephalisation)
  • Pleural effusions (often bilateral)

Hilar enlargement

  • Lymphadenopathy (TB, sarcoid, lymphoma, malignancy)
  • Vascular (PH)
  • Unilateral: consider TB, lung cancer
  • Bilateral: sarcoid, lymphoma, mets

5. Common Clinical Scenarios

Acute dyspnoea

  • Pneumothorax (absent markings, lung edge)
  • Pneumonia (consolidation, air bronchograms)
  • Pulmonary oedema (bat-wing, effusions, cardiomegaly)
  • PE (often normal; Westermark sign, Hampton's hump)
  • Pleural effusion (blunting, meniscus)

Chronic cough

  • Mass, nodule (consider lung cancer)
  • Lymphadenopathy (TB, sarcoid, lymphoma)
  • Fibrosis (upper: TB, sarcoid; lower: IPF)

Haemoptysis

  • Mass, nodule (cancer)
  • Cavitation (TB, aspergilloma, cancer)
  • Consolidation (pneumonia)
  • Bronchiectasis (tramlines, ring shadows)

6. Silhouette Sign

Concept

  • Loss of normal silhouette between adjacent structures of similar density
  • Localises disease

Examples

Lost silhouetteLobe involved
Right heart borderRight middle lobe
Left heart borderLingula
Right hemidiaphragmRight lower lobe
Left hemidiaphragmLeft lower lobe
Aortic knobLeft upper lobe (apicoposterior)
SVCRight upper lobe

7. Red Flags on CXR

  • Pneumothorax (especially tension)
  • Mediastinal shift (mass effect, tension)
  • Mass or nodule (especially >2 cm, growing)
  • New pleural effusion (esp. unilateral)
  • Lymphadenopathy (esp. mediastinal)
  • Wide mediastinum (dissection, mass)
  • Pneumomediastinum (subcutaneous emphysema)

8. Common Pitfalls

  • Poor inspiration → apparent cardiomegaly
  • Rotation → mediastinal shift
  • Skin folds, hair, clothing → mimic PTX, mass
  • Breast shadows → apparent opacities
  • Missed retrocardiac lesions (LLL behind heart)
  • Missed apical lesions (need apical views)
  • Missed subdiaphragmatic free air (perforation)
  • Missed bone lesions (ribs, spine)

9. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

DomainKey points
ApproachA-B-C-D-E-F-G-H systematic
QualityRotation, inspiration, penetration
PA vs APPA preferred (heart size)
PneumothoraxAbsent markings, lung edge
EffusionBlunting, meniscus
ConsolidationAir bronchograms, silhouette
CollapseVolume loss, mediastinal shift
Heart failureCardiomegaly, bat-wing, effusions
SilhouetteLocalises disease
CompareAlways with prior

10. MCQs (10)

  1. The most systematic approach to CXR:

A. Random

B. A-B-C-D-E-F-G-H (airway, bones, cardiac, diaphragm, effusion, fields, gastric, hilar)

C. Quick glance

D. None

E. Reverse

Answer: B — A-B-C-D-E-F-G-H.

  1. The cardiothoracic ratio on PA CXR should be:

A. >0.5

B. <0.5 (normal)

C. >0.6

D. 1.0

E. None

Answer: B — <0.5.

  1. Loss of the right heart border silhouette suggests:

A. LLL

B. Right middle lobe consolidation

C. RUL

D. None

E. LUL

Answer: B — RML.

  1. Pneumothorax is best seen on CXR as:

A. Absent lung markings + visible visceral pleural line

B. Cardiomegaly

C. Effusion

D. None

E. Mass

Answer: A — Absent markings.

  1. The most reliable sign of consolidation on CXR is:

A. Effusion

B. Air bronchogram

C. Mass

D. None

E. Cavitation

Answer: B — Air bronchogram.

  1. Bat-wing pulmonary oedema pattern on CXR is associated with:

A. PE

B. Cardiogenic pulmonary oedema

C. Pneumonia

D. None

E. ARDS

Answer: B — APO.

  1. Hampton's hump on CXR suggests:

A. Pneumonia

B. Pulmonary infarction (PE)

C. Mass

D. None

E. Effusion

Answer: B — Pulmonary infarct.

  1. Westermark sign on CXR suggests:

A. Pneumonia

B. Pulmonary embolism (oligaemia)

C. Mass

D. None

E. Effusion

Answer: B — PE.

  1. Loss of the left heart border silhouette suggests:

A. Lingular consolidation

B. RML

C. LLL

D. None

E. RUL

Answer: A — Lingula.

  1. The most common cause of unilateral white-out on CXR is:

A. Mass

B. Large pleural effusion (or lobar collapse)

C. Pneumonia

D. None

E. Pneumothorax

Answer: B — Effusion or collapse.

11. SBA Questions (10)

  1. The most common cause of bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy:

A. Sarcoidosis (in young)

B. TB

C. Lung cancer

D. None

E. Lymphoma

Answer: A — Sarcoid (in young).

  1. The first sign of left lower lobe collapse:

A. Sail sign behind heart (triangular opacity)

B. Mediastinal shift

C. Mass

D. None

E. Cavitation

Answer: A — Sail sign.

  1. The most reliable sign of LUL collapse:

A. Mass

B. Veil sign (hazy opacity)

C. Effusion

D. None

E. Cavitation

Answer: B — Veil sign.

  1. CXR quality: at least ___ anterior ribs above diaphragm:

A. 3

B. 6 (good inspiration)

C. 10

D. 1

E. None

Answer: B — 6.

  1. A cavitating lesion on CXR suggests:

A. Asthma

B. TB, lung cancer (esp. squamous), abscess, aspergilloma

C. COPD

D. None

E. PE

Answer: B — Cavitating.

  1. The most common cause of mediastinal widening on CXR:

A. Aortic aneurysm / dissection / lymphadenopathy

B. Asthma

C. COPD

D. None

E. PE

Answer: A — Aortic/lymph nodes.

  1. A coin lesion on CXR is:

A. Asthma

B. Solitary pulmonary nodule (<3 cm)

C. COPD

D. None

E. PE

Answer: B — SPN.

  1. The first sign of pulmonary oedema on CXR is:

A. Effusion

B. Upper lobe diversion (cephalisation)

C. Mass

D. None

E. Cavitation

Answer: B — Upper lobe diversion.

  1. The most useful projection for retrocardiac lesions:

A. PA

B. Lateral

C. AP

D. None

E. Lordotic

Answer: B — Lateral.

  1. The first sign of tension pneumothorax on CXR:

A. Lung edge

B. Mediastinal shift + diaphragmatic depression

C. Effusion

D. None

E. Mass

Answer: B — Mediastinal shift.

12. Flashcards

  • Q: Systematic CXR approach?

A: A-B-C-D-E-F-G-H (airway, bones, cardiac, diaphragm, effusion, fields, gastric, hilar).

  • Q: Cardiothoracic ratio?

A: <0.5 (normal).

  • Q: RML collapse silhouette?

A: Loss of right heart border.

  • Q: LLL collapse sign?

A: Sail sign behind heart.

  • Q: LUL collapse?

A: Veil sign.

  • Q: Tension PTX sign?

A: Mediastinal shift + diaphragm depression.

  • Q: Pulmonary oedema?

A: Cardiomegaly, bat-wing, effusions, upper lobe diversion.

  • Q: Hampton hump?

A: Pulmonary infarct (PE).

  • Q: Westermark?

A: Oligaemia (PE).

  • Q: SPN?

A: Solitary nodule <3 cm.

13. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. B 2. B 3. B 4. A 5. B 6. B 7. B 8. B 9. A 10. B

SBAs

  1. A 2. A 3. B 4. B 5. B 6. A 7. B 8. B 9. B 10. B

14. Summary

CXR: systematic A-B-C-D-E-F-G-H approach. Compare with prior. Quality: rotation, inspiration, penetration. Key patterns: PTX (absent markings), effusion (blunting), consolidation (air bronchogram), collapse (volume loss), oedema (bat-wing, cardiomegaly), mass/nodule. Silhouette sign localises disease. Don't miss PTX, retrocardiac lesions, apices.

15. Local Navigation

  • Chapter MOC: [[../Respiratory MOC|Respiratory MOC]]
  • Related: [[CT chest pattern approach]] · [[Pneumonia]] · [[Pleural Effusion]] · [[Pneumothorax]] · [[Lung Cancer]]

topics.title: Oxygen Therapy And Niv
topics.html:
Related: [[Respiratory Failure]], [[ABG Interpretation]], [[Acute Medicine and Critical Illness/Oxygen Therapy and NIV|Oxygen Therapy and NIV]], [[Respiratory Failure and Ventilatory Support/Non-Invasive Ventilation (NIV)|NIV]]

[!important]
Oxygen is a drug — prescribe with target saturation, flow rate, and delivery device. NIV = first-line for hypercapnic respiratory failure (AECOPD, cardiogenic pulmonary oedema, immunocompromised). Key FCPS/MRCP: target SpO₂ (88-92% at risk CO₂ retention, 94-98% otherwise), NIV indications/contraindications, settings (EPAP/IPAP), escalation to intubation.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Prescribe oxygen with target saturation, device, and flow rate
  • Identify NIV indications, contraindications, and initial settings
  • Apply NIV monitoring and escalation criteria
  • Differentiate oxygen delivery devices and their FiO₂ ranges
  • Recognise NIV failure and escalate to intubation

2. Oxygen Therapy

Target Saturations (BTS Guidelines)

Patient GroupTarget SpO₂Rationale
At risk of hypercapnia (COPD, obesity, neuromuscular, CF, severe kyphoscoliosis)88–92%Avoid hyperoxia-induced hypoventilation / V/Q mismatch
All other patients94–98%Standard oxygenation target
Critical illness / cardiac arrest / carbon monoxide / anaphylaxis94–98% (or 100% initially)Maximise oxygen delivery
Never aim for 100% SpO₂ routinely — risk of absorption atelectasis, hyperoxia toxicity.

Oxygen Delivery Devices

DeviceFlow RateApprox. FiO₂Indications
Nasal cannula1–6 L/min0.24–0.44Low-flow, stable patients
Simple face mask5–10 L/min0.40–0.60Short-term, higher flow needed
Non-rebreather mask (NRB)10–15 L/min0.85–0.95High O₂ requirement, pre-intubation
High-flow nasal oxygen (HFNO)Up to 60 L/min0.21–1.00 (titratable)Acute hypoxaemic failure, post-extubation, pre-oxygenation
Venturi maskFixed (device-specific)0.24, 0.28, 0.35, 0.40, 0.50, 0.60Precise FiO₂ (CO₂ retention risk, target 88-92%)
Tracheostomy mask / T-pieceVariableVariableTracheostomy patients
Venturi mask = only device delivering precise FiO₂ — essential for CO₂ retention risk (target 88-92%).

Oxygen Prescription (The "Drug" Prescription)

ComponentExample
Target SpO₂88–92% (COPD) / 94–98% (other)
DeviceVenturi mask 28% / Nasal cannula 2 L/min / NRB 15 L/min
Flow rateAs per device
Weaning planReduce FiO₂/flow as SpO₂ permits
MonitoringContinuous SpO₂; ABG if deteriorating / at risk CO₂ retention
O₂ on drug chart: Target SpO₂, device, flow, weaning plan.

Oxygen Toxicity & Safety

RiskPrevention
Absorption atelectasis (100% O₂)Use lowest FiO₂ to achieve target
Hyperoxia-induced vasoconstrictionTarget SpO₂ 88-92% in CO₂ retainers
CO₂ retention (Haldane effect)Target SpO₂ 88-92% in COPD; avoid overshoot
Oxygen toxicity (lungs)FiO₂ >0.6 prolonged → ARDS risk; minimise duration
Fire hazardNo smoking/open flames near O₂

Humidification

  • Low flow (<4 L/min): not required (nasal cannula)
  • High flow (>4 L/min) or prolonged: heated humidifier required
  • HFNO: built-in active humidification (37°C, 100% RH)

3. Non-Invasive Ventilation (NIV)

Definition

NIV = ventilatory support via mask/interface without endotracheal intubation. Modes: CPAP (continuous positive airway pressure) or BiPAP (bilevel positive airway pressure = IPAP + EPAP).

Indications (Strong Evidence)

ConditionNIV ModeKey Target
AECOPD (pH 7.25–7.35, PaCO₂ >6.5 kPa)BiPAPpH >7.30, RR ↓, PaCO₂ ↓
Cardiogenic pulmonary oedemaCPAP (or BiPAP)SpO₂ >94%, ↓ work of breathing
Immunocompromised (avoid intubation)BiPAPAvoid intubation complications
Post-extubation prevention (high risk)CPAP/BiPAPPrevent re-intubation
Obesity hypoventilation syndromeBiPAP (nocturnal)PaCO₂ normalisation
Neuromuscular / chest wallBiPAP (nocturnal)Symptom control, survival
Post-operative (abdominal/thoracic)CPAP/BiPAPPrevent atelectasis, re-intubation

Contraindications

AbsoluteRelative
Cardiac / respiratory arrestConfused/agitated (unable to cooperate)
Facial trauma / burnsCopious secretions (aspiration risk)
Upper airway obstructionRecent upper GI surgery (aspiration)
Unable to protect airway (GCS <8)Severe claustrophobia
Life-threatening hypoxaemia (requiring intubation)Recent facial/ocular surgery
Facial deformity (prevents seal)Recent pneumothorax (relative)

NIV Settings (BiPAP) — Typical Starting Values

ParameterStarting ValueTitration
EPAP4–5 cmH₂O↑ by 2 cmH₂O if hypoxaemia / OSA
IPAP10–12 cmH₂O (ΔP = 6–8 cmH₂O)↑ by 2–4 cmH₂O if hypercapnia persists
Backup Rate12–15/minAs needed
Inspiratory Time0.8–1.2 sAdjust for comfort
Rise Time200–300 msFaster if high drive
FiO₂Titrate to target SpO₂88-92% (CO₂ retainers) / 94-98%
Initial ΔP (IPAP-EPAP) = 6–8 cmH₂O for AECOPD; titrate to pH/PaCO₂.

NIV Monitoring (First 1-2 Hours Critical)

ParameterTargetFrequency
pH>7.30 (AECOPD)30-60 min
PaCO₂↓ >20% from baseline / <6.5 kPa30-60 min
Respiratory Rate↓ by ≥25%15-30 min
SpO₂Target per groupContinuous
Heart Rate / BPStable15-30 min
Comfort / LeakMinimal leak (<30 L/min)Continuous

NIV Failure Criteria (Escalate to Intubation)

CriterionThreshold
pH<7.25 after 1-2h NIV
PaCO₂Not falling / rising after 1-2h
Respiratory RateNot decreasing / >35/min
GCS<12 / decreasing
Haemodynamic instabilitySBP <90, arrhythmia
Inability to tolerate maskClaustrophobia, secretions
Persistent hypoxaemiaSpO₂ <88% despite FiO₂ 1.0
Do NOT delay intubation if failing — NIV delay increases mortality.

CPAP vs BiPAP

FeatureCPAPBiPAP
PressureSingle continuous pressureIPAP + EPAP (two levels)
Primary EffectSplint airways open, ↑ FRC, recruit alveoliAssist inspiration (↓ work of breathing) + splint airways
IndicationCardiogenic pulmonary oedema, OSA, post-extubationAECOPD, hypercapnic failure, neuromuscular, obesity hypoventilation
Work of BreathingNo inspiratory supportReduces inspiratory work

[Truncated]

topics.title: Spirometry Interpretation
topics.html:
Related: [[ABG Interpretation]], [[COPD]], [[Asthma]], [[Interstitial Lung Disease]], [[Airway Diseases]]

[!important]
Spirometry = cornerstone of respiratory diagnosis. Key FCPS/MRCP: FEV₁/FVC ratio (LLN vs fixed 0.7), obstructive vs restrictive vs mixed, reversibility testing, flow-volume loop patterns, severity grading (GOLD, asthma control).

1. Learning Objectives

  • Interpret spirometry systematically (Quality → Pattern → Severity → Reversibility)
  • Distinguish obstructive, restrictive, and mixed patterns
  • Apply LLN vs fixed ratio for obstruction
  • Interpret flow-volume loops
  • Apply reversibility criteria (bronchodilator, steroid trial)
  • Grade severity (GOLD, asthma control)

2. Quality Criteria (ATS/ERS)

ParameterAcceptableRepeatability
FVC≥3 acceptable manoeuvres2 best FVC within 150 mL (or 100 mL if FVC <1L)
FEV₁≥3 acceptable manoeuvres2 best FEV₁ within 150 mL (or 100 mL if FEV₁ <1L)
Start of testBack-extrapolated volume <5% FVC or 150 mL—
End of testPlateau ≥1 sec or no volume change ≥1 sec—
Cough/glottis closureNone in first second—
Unacceptable quality → results unreliable; repeat or note limitations.

3. Key Spirometric Indices

IndexDefinitionNormal
FVCForced Vital Capacity>80% predicted
FEV₁Forced Expiratory Volume in 1st second>80% predicted
FEV₁/FVCRatio>0.7 (or LLN)
FEF₂₅₋₇₅Forced Expiratory Flow 25-75%>65% predicted
PEFPeak Expiratory Flow>80% predicted
FIFForced Inspiratory Flow—
MVVMaximum Voluntary Ventilation>80% predicted

4. Reference Values

  • Predicted values: GLI (Global Lung Function Initiative) 2012 — age, sex, height, ethnicity
  • LLN (Lower Limit of Normal) = 5th percentile of predicted distribution
  • Fixed ratio (0.7) vs LLN: LLN preferred (avoids overdiagnosis in elderly, underdiagnosis in young)
FCPS/MRCP tip: LLN is gold standard for obstruction; fixed 0.7 overdiagnoses in >65, underdiagnoses in <40.

5. Systematic Interpretation (5 Steps)

1. Quality Check

  • 3 acceptable curves, FVC/FEV₁ repeatability ≤150 mL
  • If poor quality → "suboptimal, interpret with caution"

2. Identify Pattern

PatternFEV₁/FVCFVCFEV₁Typical Diseases
Normal>LLN (or >0.7)>80% pred>80% pred—
Obstructive (or <0.7)Normal/↑↓↓Asthma, COPD, bronchiectasis, CF
RestrictiveNormal/↑ (>LLN)↓↓↓ (proportional)ILD, chest wall, neuromuscular, obesity
Mixed↓↓↓↓COPD + ILD, kyphoscoliosis + COPD
FEV₁/FVC ; FVC

3. Severity Grading (If Obstructive / Restrictive)

Obstruction (COPD — GOLD)

GOLD GradeFEV₁ % Predicted
1 (Mild)≥80%
2 (Moderate)50–79%
3 (Severe)30–49%
4 (Very Severe)<30%

Restriction

GradeFVC % Predicted
Mild70–79%
Moderate50–69%
Severe<50%

4. Reversibility Testing

Bronchodilator Reversibility (BDR)

  • Standard: 400 µg salbutamol (4 puffs via spacer) → repeat spirometry at 15 min
  • Significant reversibility = FEV₁ increase ≥12% AND ≥200 mL from baseline
  • Asthma: typically >12% + ≥200 mL (often >400 mL)
  • COPD: may show some reversibility (but <12% / <200 mL typical)

Corticosteroid Trial (if BDR negative)

  • Prednisolone 30-40 mg/day × 2 weeks → repeat spirometry
  • Positive: FEV₁ increase ≥12% + ≥200 mL or PEF variability >20%

5. Flow-Volume Loop Patterns

PatternAppearanceDiagnosis
NormalSmooth expiratory curve, triangular inspiratoryNormal
ObstructiveConcave ("scooped") expiratory limb; reduced PEFAsthma, COPD, bronchiectasis
RestrictiveSmall, symmetrical loop; reduced FVC, preserved shapeILD, chest wall, neuromuscular
Fixed upper airway obstructionFlattened inspiratory AND expiratory plateauTracheal stenosis, goitre
Variable extrathoracic obstructionFlattened inspiratory limb onlyVocal cord dysfunction, laryngeal tumour
Variable intrathoracic obstructionFlattened expiratory limb onlyTracheomalacia, intrathoracic tumour

[Truncated]

topics.title: Bronchial Carcinoid Tumor
topics.html:
Related: [[Thoracic Malignancy]], [[Lung cancer]], [[Carcinoid syndrome]], [[MEN1]], [[SCLC]], [[Pulmonary neuroendocrine tumours]], [[Post-obstructive pneumonia]]

[!important]
Bronchial carcinoid tumor = well-differentiated neuroendocrine tumour (NET) of bronchial origin. Low-grade (typical) vs intermediate-grade (atypical). Key FCPS/MRCP: Young adults, central/endobronchial, cough, haemoptysis, wheeze, post-obstructive pneumonia, carcinoid syndrome rare (<5%), MEN1 association, Octreotide scan / Ga-68 DOTATATE PET, Surgical resection curative (typical), Atypical = higher malignant potential, Carcinoid syndrome = flushing, diarrhoea, valvular heart disease.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Define bronchial carcinoid and classify as typical vs atypical (WHO)
  • Recognise clinical presentations (obstructive symptoms, carcinoid syndrome, incidental)
  • Apply diagnostic imaging (CT, Octreotide scan, Ga-68 DOTATATE PET, bronchoscopy)
  • Interpret biochemistry (serum chromogranin A, 5-HIAA, serotonin)
  • Apply management (surgical resection = cornerstone, lymph node dissection, adjuvant therapy for atypical)
  • Recognise carcinoid syndrome management (octreotide, telotristat, avoid triggers)
  • Screen for MEN1 (pituitary, parathyroid, pancreatic NETs)

2. Definition

Bronchial carcinoid tumor = well-differentiated neuroendocrine tumour (NET) arising from bronchial Kulchitsky (neuroendocrine) cells. Part of pulmonary neuroendocrine tumour spectrum along with small cell lung cancer (SCLC) and large cell neuroendocrine carcinoma (LCNEC).

FCPS/MRCP tip: Carcinoids are NETs. Typical = low-grade (G1), Atypical = intermediate-grade (G2). Not small cell (high-grade G3).

3. Classification (WHO 2021)

TypeGradeMitoses (/2mm²)NecrosisKi-67Metastatic Potential
Typical carcinoid (TC)G1 (Low)<2Absent<5%Low (~10-15%)
Atypical carcinoid (AC)G2 (Intermediate)2–10Focal (punctate)5–20%Moderate (~30-50%)
FCPS/MRCP tip: Typical = <2 mitoses, no necrosis. Atypical = 2-10 mitoses and/or focal necrosis. Ki-67 not required for grading but supportive.

Comparison with Other Pulmonary NETs

TumourGradeMitosesDifferentiationPrognosis
Typical carcinoidG1<2Well-differentiatedExcellent (5-yr OS >90%)
Atypical carcinoidG22-10Well-differentiatedGood (5-yr OS 60-80%)
Large cell NE carcinoma (LCNEC)G3>10Poorly differentiatedPoor
Small cell lung cancer (SCLC)G3HighPoorly differentiatedPoor

4. Epidemiology & Risk Factors

  • Age: Young adults (30-50y), average ~45y (younger than NSCLC)
  • Sex: Female ≈ Male (slight female predominance)
  • Smoking: NOT a major risk factor (unlike NSCLC/SCLC)
  • MEN1: ~5-10% of bronchial carcinoids (assess for hyperparathyroidism, pituitary adenoma, pancreatic NETs)
  • Family history: Rare, but MEN1 predisposes

5. Clinical Features

1. Obstructive Symptoms (Most Common, ~70-80%)

  • Cough (persistent, often dry or mucoid)
  • Haemoptysis (streaky to massive, from friable vascular tumour)
  • Wheeze (monophonic, fixed, unilateral — "carcinoid wheeze")
  • Recurrent post-obstructive pneumonia (same lobe)
  • Dyspnoea (if significant obstruction)

2. Carcinoid Syndrome (Rare, <5% of Bronchial Carcinoids)

  • Flushing (face, neck, upper chest — dry, not sweating)
  • Diarrhoea (watery, secretory)
  • Bronchospasm (wheeze)
  • Valvular heart disease (right-sided: tricuspid regurgitation, pulmonary stenosis)
  • Carcinoid crisis (severe hypotension, bronchospasm, flushing — perioperative stress)
FCPS/MRCP tip: Carcinoid syndrome = hepatic metastases (liver bypasses first-pass metabolism of 5-HT). Bronchial carcinoids drain to systemic circulation directly → syndrome can occur WITHOUT liver mets (unlike GI carcinoids).

3. Cushing Syndrome (Ectopic ACTH)

  • Rapid onset, hypokalaemia, metabolic alkalosis, hypertension, hyperglycaemia
  • More common with atypical carcinoids

4. Incidental Finding

  • Asymptomatic nodule on CXR/CT for other reasons

6. Investigations

1. Imaging

ModalityFindings
CXRCentral nodule/mass (often hilar/perihilar), post-obstructive collapse/consolidation, calcifications (10-20%)
CT ChestWell-defined central mass, enhancement, calcification, lymph nodes, distal collapse
Octreotide Scan (Somatostatin Receptor Scintigraphy)Positive (SSTR2+), whole-body staging
Ga-68 DOTATATE PET/CTSuperior sensitivity/specificity (gold standard for NET staging)
FDG PET/CTVariable (typical carcinoids often FDG-negative; atypical may be positive)

2. Bronchoscopy

  • Endobronchial mass (vascular, friable, "cherry-red")
  • Biopsy — diagnostic (histology + IHC)
  • Assess resectability (airway involvement, carinal distance)

3. Biochemistry (Neuroendocrine Markers)

MarkerSignificance
Serum Chromogranin AGeneral NET marker, elevated in carcinoids (sensitivity ~80%)
24h Urinary 5-HIAASerotonin metabolite, elevated in carcinoid syndrome (specificity high)
Serum SerotoninDirect measurement
Neuron-specific Enolase (NSE)Non-specific, may be elevated

4. Histology & IHC (Diagnostic Gold Standard)

FeatureTypicalAtypical
MorphologyOrganoid nesting, trabecular, rosettesSimilar + mitoses/necrosis
IHCSynaptophysin+, Chromogranin A+, CD56+, TTF-1-, p40-Same
Ki-67<5%5-20%
Mitoses (/2mm²)<22-10
NecrosisAbsentFocal (punctate)

5. Staging (TNM 8th Edition — same as NSCLC)

StageDescription
ITumour ≤3cm, no nodes/mets
II>3cm or nodal (N1)
IIIN2/N3 or T3/T4
IVDistant mets

[Truncated — see bronchial-carcinoid-tumor.md for full content]

topics.title: Malignant Central Airway Obstruction
topics.html:
[!important]
Malignant central airway obstruction (CAO) is a complication of lung cancer, metastatic disease, or airway tumours causing significant airway narrowing. It presents with dyspnoea, stridor, monophonic wheeze, post-obstructive pneumonia. Treatment is urgent airway intervention (rigid bronchoscopy with stenting, laser, debulking) plus tumour-directed therapy.

Related: [[Lung Cancer]], [[Superior vena cava obstruction|SVC obstruction]], [[Pancoast tumor and local invasion syndromes|Pancoast]], [[Paraneoplastic pulmonary syndromes|Paraneoplastic]]

[!tip] FCPS/MRCP pearl: Malignant CAO = oncologic emergency. Most common cause: lung cancer (esp. squamous, small cell). Treatment: rigid bronchoscopy + stenting +/− laser/debulking + tumour Rx (RT, chemo, immunotherapy). Always consider in cancer patient with new dyspnoea, stridor, or recurrent post-obstructive pneumonia.

1. Definition

Malignant central airway obstruction (CAO) = significant narrowing of the trachea, main bronchi, or lobar bronchi by malignant tumour (primary or metastatic).

Severity

  • <50% narrowing: usually asymptomatic
  • 50–70%: symptomatic with exertion
  • >70%: severe, often at rest
  • Critical: near-complete, life-threatening

2. Aetiology

Primary lung cancer (most common)

  • Squamous cell carcinoma (most common — central, endobronchial)
  • Small cell lung cancer (central)
  • Adenocarcinoma (less common — usually peripheral)
  • Carcinoid (endobronchial, young patients)
  • Adenoid cystic carcinoma, mucoepidermoid (rare, central)

Metastatic / other

  • Renal cell carcinoma (endobronchial mets)
  • Breast, colon, melanoma (rare)
  • Thyroid carcinoma (extension)
  • Lymphoma (mediastinal)
  • Carcinoid (low-grade, central)
  • Kaposi sarcoma (HIV)
  • Mucoepidermoid (rare)

3. Pathophysiology

Mechanism of obstruction

  • Endobronchial tumour (most common)
  • Extrinsic compression (mediastinal mass, lymph nodes)
  • Mixed
  • Mucus plugging (above obstruction)

Consequences

  • Dyspnoea (obstruction)
  • Post-obstructive pneumonia (recurrent, same lobe)
  • Atelectasis (distal collapse)
  • Haemoptysis
  • Stridor (upper trachea)
  • Respiratory failure (severe)
  • Death (untreated)

4. Clinical Features

Symptoms

  • Dyspnoea (progressive, often acute exacerbation)
  • Stridor (tracheal obstruction)
  • Wheeze (monophonic, fixed, localised)
  • Stridor worse with supine
  • Cough (irritation)
  • Haemoptysis (mucosal erosion)
  • Recurrent pneumonia (same lobe)
  • Fever (post-obstructive pneumonia)

Signs

  • Stridor (audible)
  • Monophonic wheeze (fixed, not responsive to bronchodilators)
  • ↓Breath sounds distally
  • Cyanosis (severe)
  • Use of accessory muscles
  • Respiratory failure (severe)

5. Diagnosis

Investigations

TestPurpose
CXRAtelectasis, mass, mediastinal widening
CT chestGold standard — extent, location, severity
Bronchoscopy (rigid preferred)Visualise, biopsy, therapeutic (stent, debulk)
SpirometryFlow-volume loop (variable intrathoracic obstruction)
PET-CTStaging
BiopsyHistology + molecular

Flow-volume loop

  • Variable intrathoracic obstruction (main bronchus): flattened expiratory limb
  • Variable extrathoracic (trachea): flattened inspiratory limb
  • Fixed: both limbs flattened

6. Management

Emergency airway intervention

  • Rigid bronchoscopy (gold standard for intervention)
  • Mechanical debulking (core-out)
  • Laser (Nd:YAG)
  • Electrocautery / argon plasma coagulation (APC)
  • Cryotherapy
  • Stenting (silicone or metallic)
  • Photodynamic therapy
  • Balloon dilatation (temporary)

Tumour-directed therapy

  • External beam radiotherapy (most common)
  • Brachytherapy (endobronchial)
  • Chemotherapy (SCLC, sensitive NSCLC)
  • Targeted therapy (EGFR, ALK)
  • Immunotherapy (PD-1/PD-L1)

Supportive

  • O₂ (target SpO₂ 94–98%)
  • Corticosteroids (reduce oedema around tumour)
  • Antibiotics (post-obstructive pneumonia)
  • Humidification
  • Pulmonary rehabilitation

Specific by histology

TumourTreatment
SCLCChemo (cisplatin + etoposide), RT
NSCLC squamousRT +/− chemo, immunotherapy
CarcinoidSurgical resection (preferred), bronchoscopic if surgical not possible
Adenoid cysticSurgery + RT
MetastaticTreat primary +/− local Rx

7. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

DomainKey points
DefinitionMalignant central airway narrowing
Most common causeLung cancer (squamous, SCLC)
SymptomsDyspnoea, stridor, monophonic wheeze, post-obstructive pneumonia
Best imagingCT chest + bronchoscopy
TreatmentRigid bronchoscopy + stenting + tumour Rx
PrognosisPoor without intervention
Tumour RxRT, chemo, targeted, immunotherapy

8. MCQs (10)

  1. The most common cause of malignant central airway obstruction is:

A. Carcinoid

B. Lung cancer (esp. squamous)

C. Metastasis

D. None

E. Lymphoma

Answer: B — Lung cancer.

  1. The best investigation for malignant CAO is:

A. CT chest + bronchoscopy

B. CXR

C. Spirogram

D. ABG

E. None

Answer: A — CT + bronchoscopy.

  1. The first-line intervention for malignant CAO is:

A. Steroids

B. Rigid bronchoscopy +/− stent + tumour Rx

C. Wait

D. Antibiotics

E. None

Answer: B — Bronchoscopic intervention.

  1. The most common lung cancer histology for CAO is:

A. Squamous cell carcinoma

B. Adenocarcinoma

C. Large cell

D. None

E. Carcinoid

Answer: A — Squamous.

  1. The flow-volume loop in fixed central obstruction shows:

A. Flattening of both inspiratory and expiratory limbs

B. Normal

C. Restrictive

D. None

E. Variable

Answer: A — Both limbs.

  1. A 60-year-old with known lung cancer has new stridor, monophonic wheeze. Cause:

A. Central airway obstruction

B. Pneumonia

C. Pleural effusion

D. None

E. Asthma

Answer: A — CAO.

  1. The most useful bronchoscopic intervention for malignant CAO:

A. Biopsy only

B. Mechanical debulking + laser + stent

C. Lavage

D. None

E. Brushing

Answer: B — Debulking + laser + stent.

  1. The most common treatment of malignant CAO due to SCLC:

A. Stent

B. Chemoradiotherapy

C. Surgery

D. None

E. Antibiotics

Answer: B — ChemoRT.

  1. Carcinoid tumour causing CAO is best treated by:

A. Stent

B. Surgical resection

C. Wait

D. Steroids

E. None

Answer: B — Surgery.

  1. The most useful treatment for recurrent post-obstructive pneumonia in cancer:

A. Antibiotics only

B. Bronchoscopy with intervention +/− stent

C. Steroids

D. None

E. Wait

Answer: B — Bronchoscopic intervention.

9. SBA Questions (10)

  1. The most common cause of stridor in cancer patient:

A. PTX

B. Malignant CAO (tracheal)

C. Pleural effusion

D. None

E. Pneumonia

Answer: B — CAO.

  1. The best initial treatment for symptomatic malignant CAO:

A. Wait

B. Bronchoscopic intervention (stent, laser, debulk)

C. Steroids

D. Antibiotics

E. None

Answer: B — Bronchoscopic.

  1. The most common histology of central airway tumours:

A. Squamous cell carcinoma

B. Adenocarcinoma

C. SCLC

D. None

E. Carcinoid

Answer: A — Squamous.

  1. A patient with lung cancer and recurrent lobar pneumonia. Cause:

A. Aspiration

B. Endobronchial obstruction (CAO)

C. None

D. Bronchiectasis

E. COPD

Answer: B — CAO.

  1. The most useful imaging for staging of malignant CAO:

A. CT chest with contrast

B. CXR

C. Spirogram

D. None

E. MRI

Answer: A — CT.

  1. Rigid bronchoscopy is preferred over flexible for:

A. Biopsy

B. Therapeutic intervention (stent, laser, debulking)

C. Lavage

D. None

E. Brushing

Answer: B — Therapeutic.

  1. The most common airway complication of lung cancer:

A. Massive haemoptysis

B. Malignant central airway obstruction

C. PTX

D. None

E. Pleural effusion

Answer: B — CAO.

  1. The best palliation for endobronchial tumour causing post-obstructive pneumonia:

A. Wait

B. Stent + antibiotics + tumour Rx

C. Steroids

D. None

E. Surgery

Answer: B — Stent + Rx.

  1. The flow-volume loop in variable intrathoracic obstruction (main bronchus):

A. Flattened expiratory limb

B. Normal

C. Both limbs

D. None

E. Inspiratory

Answer: A — Expiratory limb.

  1. A 50-year-old with central squamous tumour, severe dyspnoea. Best first:

A. Steroids

B. Rigid bronchoscopy + tumour debulking + stent

C. Wait

D. Chemo

E. None

Answer: B — Bronchoscopic intervention.

10. Flashcards

  • Q: Most common cause of malignant CAO?

A: Lung cancer (squamous).

  • Q: Best investigation?

A: CT + bronchoscopy.

  • Q: First-line intervention?

A: Rigid bronchoscopy + stent + tumour Rx.

  • Q: Most common histology?

A: Squamous.

  • Q: Fixed CAO flow-volume?

A: Both limbs flattened.

  • Q: Carcinoid CAO Rx?

A: Surgery.

  • Q: SCLC CAO Rx?

A: ChemoRT.

  • Q: Bronchoscopic options?

A: Debulking, laser, stent, cryo, APC.

  • Q: Recurrent post-obstructive pneumonia?

A: Bronchoscopic intervention.

  • Q: Most common symptom?

A: Dyspnoea.

11. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. B 2. A 3. B 4. A 5. A 6. A 7. B 8. B 9. B 10. B

SBAs

  1. B 2. B 3. A 4. B 5. A 6. B 7. B 8. B 9. A 10. B

12. Summary

Malignant CAO = central airway narrowing by tumour (most common: lung cancer, esp. squamous). Symptoms: dyspnoea, stridor, monophonic wheeze, recurrent post-obstructive pneumonia. Best imaging: CT + bronchoscopy. Treatment: rigid bronchoscopy + stenting + tumour Rx (RT, chemo, immunotherapy). Oncologic emergency.

topics.title: Mesothelioma
topics.html:
Related: [[Malignant pleural effusion]], [[Asbestosis]], [[Lung cancer]], [[Pleural aspiration and chest drain basics]], [[Talc pleurodesis]], [[Industrial compensation]]

[!important]
Malignant pleural mesothelioma (MPM) = primary malignancy of the pleura (rarely peritoneum/pericardium). Asbestos exposure is the principal cause (latency 20–50 years). Poor prognosis (median survival 9–12 months). Key FCPS/MRCP: Asbestos link, immunohistochemistry (Calretinin+, WT1+, D2-40+, CK5/6+, BerEP4-, MOC-31-, TTF-1-), CT findings (thickened pleura, nodularity, encasement), BTS/NICE management (chemotherapy: cisplatin+pemetrexed ± bevacizumab; immunotherapy: nivolumab+ipilimumab; radiotherapy for port sites; VATS for diagnosis/palliation), benign asbestos pleural effusion (BAPE) differential, industrial compensation.

1. Learning Objectives

  • Recognise asbestos exposure history and latency period (20–50 years)
  • Interpret CT chest findings (unilateral pleural thickening, nodularity, mediastinal fixation, volume loss)
  • Differentiate MPM from metastatic adenocarcinoma using immunohistochemistry panel (Calretinin, WT1, D2-40, CK5/6 vs BerEP4, MOC-31, CEA, TTF-1, Napsin A)
  • Apply BTS/NICE staging (IMIG/TNM) and prognostic scores (EORTC, CALGB)
  • Manage with first-line chemotherapy (cisplatin/carboplatin + pemetrexed ± bevacizumab) and immunotherapy (nivolumab + ipilimumab)
  • Know radiotherapy indications (port site prophylaxis controversial, palliative for pain/chest wall)
  • Diagnose Benign Asbestos Pleural Effusion (BAPE) — diagnosis of exclusion
  • Guide patients on industrial injuries disablement benefit (UK)

2. Definition

Malignant pleural mesothelioma (MPM) = primary malignant neoplasm arising from mesothelial cells of the pleura (90% pleural, 10% peritoneal, rare pericardial/tunica vaginalis).

Incidence: ~2,500/year in UK (peaking 2020s), male:female ~5:1, median age 70–75 years.

3. Core Anatomy

1. Pleural anatomy relevant to mesothelioma

  • Visceral pleura (covers lung) and parietal pleura (lines chest wall, diaphragm, mediastinum)
  • Pleural space normally 10–20 mL serous fluid
  • Lymphatic drainage: parietal pleura → mediastinal nodes → thoracic duct
  • Mesothelial cells line both surfaces → malignant transformation

2. Spread patterns

  • Local: Direct invasion of chest wall, diaphragm, pericardium, lung parenchyma, mediastinum
  • Lymphatic: Ipsilateral then contralateral mediastinal nodes, supraclavicular
  • Haematogenous: Liver, bone, brain, adrenal (late)
  • Transdiaphragmatic: Peritoneal mesothelioma (10%)

3. Asbestos bodies

  • Asbestos fibres (amphibole > chrysotile) inhaled → reach pleural space via lymphatics or direct penetration
  • Ferruginous bodies (asbestos fibres coated in iron-protein) in lung tissue/pleural fluid = exposure marker
  • Pleural plaques (calcified, diaphragmatic, posterolateral) = exposure marker, NOT mesothelioma (but increase risk)

4. Core Physiology

Carcinogenesis

  1. Asbestos fibres (especially amphiboles: crocidolite, amosite) → phagocytosed by mesothelial cells/macrophages
  2. Frustrated phagocytosis (long fibres) → ROS, RNS, chronic inflammation
  3. Genotoxic effects: Chromosomal damage (deletions in 3p, 9p21/CDKN2A, 13q, 22q/NF2)
  4. Tumour suppressor loss: BAP1 (BRCA1-associated protein 1), CDKN2A (p16), NF2 (merlin)
  5. Latency: 20–50 years from first exposure to clinical disease

Pathophysiology of symptoms

  • Pleural thickening → restricts lung expansion → dyspnoea
  • Nodular encasement → fixes mediastinum → superior vena cava syndrome, phrenic nerve palsy
  • Pleural effusion (exudate, often haemorrhagic) → compression atelectasis
  • Chest wall invasion → pain (intercostal nerves), palpable mass
  • Diaphragmatic invasion → referred shoulder tip pain (phrenic nerve)

5. Normal Values / Important Cut-offs

Prognostic Scores

ScoreFactorsRisk Groups
EORTCStage, histology, performance status, LDH, WBC, plateletsLow/Intermediate/High (median OS 12–20 mo vs 6–9 mo)
CALGBPerformance status, histology, stage, platelets, LDH, ageSimilar
Lung Mesothelioma Prognostic Index (LMPI)Stage, histology, PS, LDH, platelets, NLRContinuous

Histological Subtypes (WHO)

SubtypeFrequencyPrognosisKey Features
Epithelioid~60%Best (median 12–24 mo)Uniform cells, tubulopapillary, glandular; Calretinin+, WT1+, CK5/6+
Sarcomatoid~10-20%Worst (median 6–9 mo)Spindle cells, desmoplastic; vimentin+, cytokeratin variable; resistant to chemo
Biphasic (mixed)~20-30%IntermediateBoth epithelioid + sarcomatoid components (>10% each)

Staging (IMIG / TNM 8th Edition)

StageDescriptionMedian Survival
IAIpsilateral parietal ± visceral pleura, no nodal/metastatic~20–30 mo
IBIA + visceral pleura involvement~18–25 mo
IIIB + ipsilateral hilar/internal mammary nodes~15–20 mo
IIIAIIIA = locally advanced (chest wall, pericardium, diaphragm) + nodes~12–16 mo
IIIBContralateral nodes, supraclavicular, extensive local invasion~10–14 mo
IVDistant metastases~6–9 mo

6. Classification

By histology (as above)

By site

  • Pleural (90%) — MPM
  • Peritoneal (10%) — often with ascites, better prognosis with CRS/HIPEC
  • Pericardial (<1%)
  • Tunica vaginalis (rare)

By asbestos association

  • Occupational (shipbuilding, construction, insulation, automotive)
  • Para-occupational (family contact via contaminated work clothes)
  • Environmental (near asbestos mines/factories)
  • Spontaneous (no identifiable exposure — rare, <5%)

7. Etiology / Causes

Asbestos Types (Risk Hierarchy)

TypeFibrePotencyUse
Crocidolite (blue)AmphiboleHighestInsulation, cement
Amosite (brown)AmphiboleHighInsulation board
Chrysotile (white)SerpentineLower (but still causative)Cement, brakes, textiles
Tremolite/Actinolite/AnthophylliteAmphiboleHighContaminants

Other Risk Factors

  • Radiation (thoracic radiotherapy for lymphoma)
  • Erionite (zeolite mineral, Turkey villages — extremely high risk)
  • SV40 virus (controversial, possibly cofactor)
  • Genetic: BAP1 germline mutation (mesothelioma predisposition syndrome — uveal melanoma, RCC, meningioma)

8. Risk Factors

  • Occupational exposure (shipyard, construction, power plants, automotive brake repair, asbestos mining/milling)
  • Duration/intensity of exposure (dose-response)
  • Latency 20–50 years (peak incidence 2020s in UK)
  • Male sex (occupational exposure pattern)
  • Age >60 (latency)
  • Smoking — NOT a direct risk factor for mesothelioma (but synergises for lung cancer)

9. Pathophysiology (Detailed)

  1. Inhalation of asbestos fibres → deposition at bifurcations, lower lobes
  2. Migration to pleural space via lymphatics or direct penetration
  3. Mesothelial cell uptake → frustrated phagocytosis (long fibres)
  4. Chronic inflammation → ROS, cytokines (TNF-α, IL-6, IL-8)
  5. Genetic damage: CDKN2A (p16) deletion 70%, NF2 mutation 40%, BAP1 loss 60%
  6. Clonal expansion → malignant mesothelial cells
  7. Local invasion → pleural thickening, nodularity, effusion
  8. Late dissemination → nodes, contralateral, distant

10. Clinical Features

History

  • Dyspnoea (progressive, effusion + restriction) — most common
  • Chest pain (pleuritic, dull, chest wall invasion) — common
  • Weight loss, anorexia, fatigue
  • Night sweats (less common than TB)
  • Asbestos exposure history (occupational, DIY, family) — critical

Examination

  • Effusion signs: dull percussion, reduced breath sounds, reduced expansion
  • Pleural thickening: palpable nodules, chest wall fixation
  • Mediastinal fixation: trachea central/pulled to affected side
  • Clubbing (uncommon, <10%)
  • Superior vena cava obstruction (late, mediastinal invasion)
  • Horner's syndrome (apical invasion)
  • Phrenic nerve palsy (diaphragmatic invasion)
  • Signs of peritoneal spread (ascites, abdominal mass)

Paraneoplastic

  • Hypoglycaemia (IGF-II secretion, rare)
  • Thrombocytosis (IL-6)
  • Hypercoagulability (Troussseau's)

11. Investigations

1. Imaging

CXR (PA erect)

  • Unilateral pleural effusion (often large, haemorrhagic)
  • Pleural thickening (diffuse, nodular, circumferential)
  • Volume loss (ipsilateral hemithorax smaller)
  • Mediastinal shift (fixed, not shifted by effusion)

CT Thorax with IV contrast — KEY INVESTIGATION

  • Pleural thickening (>1 cm, nodular, circumferential = MPM)
  • "Encasement" of lung (thickened visceral pleura)
  • Mediastinal fixation/invasion
  • Chest wall/diaphragm invasion
  • Nodal involvement (ipsilateral → contralateral)
  • Contralateral pleural disease
  • Peritoneal disease (if present)
  • Volumetric assessment (for surgery planning)

PET-CT (staging)

  • High SUV in pleural thickening/nodules
  • Detects occult nodal/distant mets (upstages 15-20%)

MRI (selected)

  • Chest wall/diaphragm invasion (better soft tissue)
  • Brachial plexus invasion (Pancoast-type)

2. Pleural Fluid Analysis

Diagnostic aspiration (US-guided)

ParameterMPM Typical
AppearanceSerosanguineous / haemorrhagic
ExudateLight's criteria positive
Cell countVariable (often neutrophilic early, lymphocytic later)
CytologyPositive ~30-40% only (low sensitivity)
BiomarkersMesothelin (↑ in epithelioid, not sarcomatoid), Fibulin-3, Osteopontin (not routine)
FCPS/MRCP tip: Fluid cytology sensitivity only 30-40% for MPM. Negative cytology does NOT exclude — need thoracoscopic biopsy.

3. Tissue Diagnosis (Gold Standard)

MethodYieldIndication
Image-guided cutting needle (US/CT)60-70%First-line if accessible, but risk of tract seeding
Thoracoscopic (VATS/LAT) biopsy>95%Preferred — multiple biopsies, direct vision, allows talc poudrage
Open biopsyRarely neededFailed VATS

4. Immunohistochemistry (CRITICAL for Differentiation)

MarkerMPM (Epithelioid)Metastatic Adenocarcinoma (Lung/Breast/GI)
CalretininPositiveNegative
WT1PositiveNegative (except ovarian/ genital)

[Content truncated for rendering — see mesothelioma.md for full content]
