topics.title: Dyspnea
topics.html:
[!important]
Dyspnoea is the subjective experience of breathing discomfort. It is one of the most common presenting symptoms in acute and chronic disease, with a wide differential spanning respiratory, cardiac, neuromuscular, metabolic, psychogenic, and haematological causes. Systematic assessment (acute vs chronic, exertional vs at rest, associated features) narrows the diagnosis.

Related: [[Respiratory Failure]], [[ABG Interpretation]], [[Oxygen Therapy and NIV]], [[Chest X-Ray Approach]], [[Spirometry Interpretation]], [[COPD]], [[Asthma]], [[Heart Failure]], [[Pneumonia]], [[Pulmonary Embolism]], [[Lung Cancer]], [[Pleural Effusion]], [[Interstitial Lung Disease]]

[!tip] FCPS/MRCP pearl: Always assess onset (acute vs chronic), triggers (exertion, position, time of day), associated features (chest pain, cough, wheeze, sputum, orthopnoea, PND, leg swelling, fatigue), and red flags (syncope, stridor, hypoxia, JVP, murmurs, calf swelling). First-line investigations: SpO₂, ABG, CXR, ECG, FBC, BNP, troponin, D-dimer (if PE suspected).

1. Definition

Dyspnoea (breathlessness) = subjective experience of breathing discomfort. Distinct from:

  • Tachypnoea (↑RR, often without distress)
  • Hyperventilation (↑minute ventilation, often with low PaCO₂)
  • Orthopnoea (breathlessness lying flat — cardiac, COPD, OSA, OHS)
  • Platypnoea (breathlessness upright — ASD, hepatopulmonary syndrome)
  • Trepopnoea (breathlessness in one lateral position — unilateral lung disease, cardiac)

Classification by onset

PatternExamples
Acute (seconds–minutes)Pneumothorax, PE, asthma attack, anaphylaxis, airway obstruction (FB, angioedema), panic attack
Acute (hours)Pneumonia, AECOPD, pulmonary oedema, ARDS, metabolic acidosis
Subacute (days–weeks)Pleural effusion, anaemia, thyrotoxicosis, NM weakness (GBS)
Chronic (months–years)COPD, ILD, chronic heart failure, obesity, anaemia, deconditioning

2. Pathophysiology

Mechanisms

  • Chemoreceptors — peripheral (carotid/aortic body, hypoxaemia) + central (medulla, hypercapnia, acidosis)
  • Mechanoreceptors — lung stretch (Hering-Breuer), J-receptors (pulmonary oedema), chest wall (muscle spindles)
  • Efferent signals — motor cortex, brainstem respiratory centres
  • Afferent mismatch — when expected ventilation ≠ actual → sense of effort

Pathophysiology by cause

  • Airway obstruction — ↑resistance → ↑work of breathing
  • Lung parenchymal disease — ↓compliance → ↑elastic work
  • Airway dead space — ↑ventilation needed
  • Cardiac — ↓cardiac output → pulmonary congestion → J-receptor stimulation
  • Anaemia — ↓O₂ carrying capacity → ↑cardiac output
  • NM weakness — ↓respiratory muscle strength
  • Psychogenic — hyperventilation, often with panic

3. Aetiology — comprehensive differential

Respiratory

CauseClue
COPDSmoking, chronic cough, ↓FEV₁/FVC, hyperinflation
AsthmaWheeze, nocturnal, BD reversibility, triggers
PneumoniaFever, productive cough, consolidation, ↑WCC/CRP
PESudden, pleuritic pain, hypoxia, A-a gradient, risk factors
PneumothoraxSudden pleuritic pain, ↓breath sounds, hyperresonance
Pleural effusionStony dull, ↓breath sounds, ↓vocal fremitus
ILDProgressive, dry cough, velcro crackles, clubbing
Lung cancerSmoking, weight loss, hemoptysis, mass
Upper airway obstructionStridor, FB, angioedema
Pulmonary oedemaOrthopnoea, PND, bilateral crackles, ↑JVP, ↑BNP
ARDSAcute, bilateral infiltrates, refractory hypoxaemia
Pulmonary hypertensionProgressive exertional, syncope, loud P2

Cardiac

  • Heart failure (HFrEF, HFpEF) — orthopnoea, PND, ↑JVP, S3, peripheral oedema
  • Acute coronary syndrome — chest pain, ECG changes
  • Valvular heart disease (AS, MS) — murmur, syncope
  • Arrhythmia — palpitation, irregular pulse
  • Pericardial effusion/tamponade — Beck's triad
  • Cardiomyopathy

Neuromuscular / chest wall

  • GBS, MG, ALS, polymyositis — proximal weakness, ↓VC
  • Kyphoscoliosis, ankylosing spondylitis — restrictive, deformed chest
  • Obesity hypoventilation syndrome — BMI >30, daytime hypercapnia
  • Diaphragm palsy — orthopnoea, paradoxical abdominal movement
  • Central hypoventilation (stroke, opioids)

Metabolic / endocrine

  • Metabolic acidosis (DKA, AKI, salicylate) — Kussmaul breathing
  • Thyrotoxicosis — weight loss, heat intolerance, tremor
  • Anaemia — pallor, fatigue, ↓Hb

Psychogenic

  • Anxiety / panic — sighing, perioral tingling, normal SpO₂
  • Somatisation

4. Clinical Assessment

History

FeatureSignificance
OnsetSudden (PTX, PE, FB) vs gradual (COPD, HF)
TriggersExertion, allergens, position, cold air, emotion
Time of dayNocturnal (asthma, HF); morning (COPD)
PositionOrthopnoea (HF), platypnoea (ASD, HPS)
SeveritymMRC dyspnoea scale, Borg scale
Associated symptomsCough, sputum, wheeze, chest pain, haemoptysis, fever, leg swelling, oedema
Past historyAsthma, COPD, HF, cancer, DVT/PE
DrugsBeta-blockers, aspirin, NSAIDs, ACEi
SmokingPack-years
OccupationAsbestos, silica, coal, birds
TravelLong flight, immobilisation (PE risk)

mMRC Dyspnoea Scale

GradeDescription
0Not troubled by breathlessness except on strenuous exercise
1Short of breath when hurrying on level ground or walking up slight hill
2Walks slower than peers on level ground, or stops for breath
3Stops for breath after walking ~100 m on level
4Too breathless to leave house, or breathless on dressing/undressing

Examination

  • General: cyanosis, pallor, cachexia, clubbing, nicotine staining
  • Vital signs: RR, HR, BP, SpO₂, temperature
  • Neck: JVP ↑ (HF, tamponade, TR), stridor, tracheal deviation
  • Chest: inspection, palpation, percussion, auscultation
  • Cardiac: murmurs, S3, S4, pericardial rub
  • Abdomen: hepatomegaly, ascites
  • Limbs: peripheral oedema, calf swelling/tenderness, cyanosis, clubbing

5. Investigations

First-line

TestUse
SpO₂All patients
ABGConfirm hypoxaemia, hypercapnia, acidosis
CXRPneumonia, oedema, effusion, pneumothorax, mass, hyperinflation
ECGIschaemia, arrhythmia, RV strain, low voltage
FBCAnaemia, infection (WCC, eosinophils)
U&E, glucoseRenal failure, DKA
CRPInfection
BNP / NT-proBNPHeart failure (high negative predictive value)
TroponinACS
D-dimerPE (low pre-test)
SpirometryObstructive vs restrictive
Peak flowAsthma severity

Second-line

  • CTPA — suspected PE
  • Echocardiogram — cardiac function, valves, PHT
  • HRCT chest — ILD
  • Sputum — culture, AFB, cytology
  • Cardiopulmonary exercise test — undifferentiated dyspnoea, exercise-induced
  • Sleep study — OSA, OHS
  • Lactate — sepsis, tissue hypoxia

6. Diagnosis — structured approach

⬇ Clinical Pathway
Dyspnoea
↓
Acute vs chronic?
↓
ABG, CXR, ECG, SpO2, D-dimer
Full history + CXR + spirometry + ABG + ECG + FBC + BNP
↓
Pneumothorax?
PE suspected?
Pneumonia/oedema?
Asthma/COPD?
Cardiac?
Metabolic?
Obstructive spirometry?
Restrictive spirometry?
Normal spirometry, low SpO2?
Cardiac cause?
↓
Chest drain
Wells/D-dimer/CTPA → treat
Treat infection/diuretics
Bronchodilators, steroids
ECG, troponin, echo, treat
ABG, lactate, treat cause
COPD/asthma workup
HRCT → ILD vs NM vs chest wall
CTPA, echo, anaemia, NM
Echo, BNP → treat HF

7. Management

General

  • Position — upright (relieves orthopnoea)
  • Oxygen — target SpO₂ 94–98% (88–92% in Type 2 risk)
  • Treat underlying cause:
  • Bronchodilators + steroids — asthma/COPD
  • Diuretics, nitrates, ACEi — pulmonary oedema
  • Anticoagulation, thrombolysis — PE
  • Antibiotics — pneumonia
  • Chest drain — pneumothorax
  • Pleural drainage — large effusion
  • Transfusion, EPO, iron — anaemia
  • CPAP, weight loss — OSA/OHS
  • NIV / intubation — respiratory failure
  • Psychological support — anxiety, palliative dyspnoea
  • Pulmonary rehabilitation — chronic dyspnoea
  • Palliative care — dyspnoea at end of life (opioids, benzodiazepines)

8. Common Viva Questions

QuestionExpected answer
What is the mMRC scale?0–4 grading of dyspnoea based on level of activity that provokes it.
Name 6 causes of acute dyspnoea.Pneumothorax, PE, pneumonia, asthma, pulmonary oedema, anaphylaxis.
Name 6 causes of chronic dyspnoea.COPD, asthma, ILD, heart failure, anaemia, obesity.
What is orthopnoea? Common causes?Breathlessness lying flat; HF, COPD, OHS, diaphragmatic palsy, bilateral diaphragmatic paralysis.
What is platypnoea?Breathlessness relieved by lying flat; ASD, hepatopulmonary syndrome, post-pneumonectomy.
What is the role of BNP?High negative predictive value; ↑BNP supports heart failure, low BNP argues against.
What investigations for chronic dyspnoea of unclear cause?CXR, spirometry, ABG, ECG, FBC, BNP, echo, CT chest (HRCT if ILD), CPET.
What is the Kussmaul sign?Deep, laboured breathing in metabolic acidosis (DKA, renal failure).
What is sighing respiration?Sighing respirations are a feature of anxiety/panic; not associated with hypoxaemia.

9. Confusions & Mnemonics

Differential of dyspnoea — "CHAPS-PAID": Cardiac (HF, ACS, tamponade), Haematology (anaemia), Airway (asthma, COPD, FB), Psychogenic (panic), Structural (PTX, effusion), Pulmonary (pneumonia, PE, ILD, cancer), Acid-base (metabolic acidosis), Idiopathic/Immune (vasculitis), Drugs (opioids, salicylates)

Orthopnoea causes — "CHOP": CHF, Hiatus hernia (large), Obesity, Paralysis (diaphragm) / Pulmonary (COPD)

Acute dyspnoea — "4 P's": PTX, PE, Pneumonia, Pulmonary oedema

Red flags — "SHOCK": Syncope, Hypoxia, Orthopnoea, Cyanosis, Kussmaul

10. Local Navigation

  • Chapter MOC: [[../Respiratory MOC|Respiratory MOC]]
  • Related: [[COPD]] · [[Asthma]] · [[Pneumonia]] · [[Pulmonary Embolism]] · [[Pneumothorax]] · [[Pleural Effusion]] · [[Lung Cancer]] · [[Interstitial Lung Disease]] · [[Respiratory Failure]] · [[ABG Interpretation]] · [[Oxygen Therapy and NIV]] · [[Chest X-Ray Approach]] · [[Spirometry Interpretation]]

11. MCQs (10)

  1. A 60-year-old ex-smoker with progressive dyspnoea has FEV₁ 60% predicted, FEV₁/FVC 0.65. Most likely diagnosis:

A. Asthma

B. COPD

C. ILD

D. Heart failure

E. PE

Answer: B — Obstructive pattern + smoking = COPD.

  1. A 30-year-old with episodic dyspnoea and wheeze, normal spirometry between attacks. Next test:

A. CXR

B. Methacholine challenge

C. CT

D. Bronchoscopy

E. V/Q scan

Answer: B — Bronchoprovocation to confirm/exclude asthma.

  1. A 50-year-old with progressive dyspnoea, dry cough, fine inspiratory crackles, ↓DLCO, ↓FVC with normal FEV₁/FVC. Pattern:

A. Obstructive

B. Restrictive

C. Mixed

D. Normal

E. Variable

Answer: B — Restrictive pattern.

  1. A 70-year-old with sudden dyspnoea, unilateral hyperresonance, ↓breath sounds. Most likely:

A. Pneumonia

B. Pneumothorax

C. PE

D. Asthma

D. Pleural effusion

Answer: B — Spontaneous PTX (thin elderly).

  1. A 40-year-old with sudden dyspnoea, pleuritic pain, hypoxia, normal CXR. Wells score 4.5. Next step:

A. D-dimer

B. CTPA

C. V/Q scan

D. Treat empirically

E. Discharge

Answer: B — Wells >4 = PE likely → CTPA.

  1. mMRC grade 3 dyspnoea means:

A. Dyspnoea on strenuous exercise

B. Dyspnoea on walking up hill

C. Stops for breath after walking ~100 m on level

D. Too breathless to leave house

E. Breathless on dressing

Answer: C — mMRC 3.

  1. Platypnoea (breathless upright) is characteristic of:

A. COPD

B. Atrial septal defect, hepatopulmonary syndrome

C. Heart failure

D. Pleural effusion

E. Pneumothorax

Answer: B — ASD/HPS.

  1. Kussmaul breathing (deep, laboured) suggests:

A. COPD

B. Metabolic acidosis (DKA, renal failure)

C. Asthma

D. PE

E. Hyperventilation

Answer: B — Compensation for metabolic acidosis.

  1. First-line investigation in chronic dyspnoea of unclear cause:

A. CXR

B. CXR + spirometry + ABG + ECG + FBC + BNP

C. CT

D. Bronchoscopy

E. Spirogram only

Answer: B — First-line panel.

  1. BNP/NT-proBNP in dyspnoea differential:

A. Non-specific

B. High NPV for heart failure; supports cardiac cause

C. Specific for COPD

D. Never used

E. Diagnostic of PE

Answer: B — High NPV for HF.

12. SBA Questions (10)

  1. A 65-year-old with progressive dyspnoea has FEV₁/FVC 0.65, FEV₁ 60%, ↑TLC, hyperinflation. Diagnosis:

A. Asthma

B. COPD

C. ILD

D. Bronchiectasis

E. PE

Answer: B — COPD.

  1. A 35-year-old with episodic dyspnoea, normal exam, normal spirometry. Best test:

A. CT

B. Methacholine challenge

C. CXR

D. Bronchoscopy

E. V/Q

Answer: B — Challenge to confirm asthma.

  1. A 50-year-old with progressive dyspnoea, dry cough, ↓DLCO (lowest sensitivity for ILD):

A. ↓DLCO is the earliest spirometric change in ILD

B. ↓FVC is earliest

C. ↓FEV₁ is earliest

D. Normal spirometry

E. All normal early

Answer: A — DLCO earliest.

  1. A 70-year-old ex-smoker with unilateral hyperresonance, ↓breath sounds. Best next step:

A. CXR

B. CXR (confirm pneumothorax)

C. CT

D. Bronchoscopy

E. Antibiotics

Answer: B — CXR confirms PTX.

  1. A 40-year-old with sudden dyspnoea, pleuritic pain, hypoxia, D-dimer 1200. Wells 4.5. Next:

A. Treat empirically

B. CTPA

C. V/Q

D. Repeat D-dimer

E. Discharge

Answer: B — CTPA.

  1. A 60-year-old with chronic dyspnoea, orthopnoea, bibasal crackles, S3, ↑BNP. Cause:

A. COPD

B. Heart failure

C. PE

D. Asthma

E. Anxiety

Answer: B — Heart failure.

  1. A 30-year-old obese (BMI 38) with daytime somnolence, ↑PaCO₂ 7.0 kPa. Diagnosis:

A. OSA only

B. Obesity hypoventilation syndrome (OHS)

C. COPD

D. Asthma

E. Cardiac

Answer: B — OHS = obesity + daytime hypercapnia.

  1. A 25-year-old with sudden dyspnoea after eating peanuts, urticaria, stridor. Action:

A. Salbutamol

B. IM adrenaline 0.5 mg (1:1000)

C. IV hydrocortisone

D. Inhaled steroids

E. Observation

Answer: B — Anaphylaxis → IM adrenaline.

  1. Trepopnoea (worse in one lateral position) suggests:

A. COPD

B. Unilateral lung disease, cardiac (e.g. ball-valve thrombus)

C. Asthma

D. Anxiety

E. Bilateral effusion

Answer: B — Unilateral lung disease.

  1. A 55-year-old with dyspnoea, normal CXR, normal spirometry, ↓DLCO, ↑A-a gradient. Most likely:

A. COPD

B. ILD or pulmonary vascular disease

C. Heart failure

D. Anxiety

E. Obesity

Answer: B — DLCO + A-a gradient with normal spirometry → ILD or PV disease.

13. Flashcards

  • Q: Most sensitive bedside sign of dyspnoea?

A: Tachypnoea (≥20/min) is the most sensitive clinical sign.

  • Q: mMRC dyspnoea scale range?

A: 0 (no dyspnoea) to 4 (breathless on dressing/undressing).

  • Q: Platypnoea definition?

A: Breathlessness worse upright (relieved by lying flat); ASD, HPS.

  • Q: Kussmaul respiration?

A: Deep, laboured breathing in metabolic acidosis (DKA, renal failure).

  • Q: Most useful test for chronic unexplained dyspnoea?

A: Combined first-line panel: CXR + spirometry + ABG + ECG + FBC + BNP.

  • Q: Role of BNP in dyspnoea?

A: High negative predictive value for heart failure; helps differentiate cardiac from pulmonary cause.

  • Q: First test for suspected ILD?

A: HRCT chest (cornerstone).

  • Q: First test for suspected PE?

A: CTPA in stable; V/Q in pregnancy/CKD.

  • Q: Orthopnoea differential?

A: Heart failure, COPD, OHS, bilateral diaphragm palsy, large hiatus hernia.

  • Q: First-line therapy for dyspnoea at end of life?

A: Opioids (morphine) ± benzodiazepines; oxygen if hypoxaemic.

14. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. B — COPD.
  2. B — Methacholine challenge.
  3. B — Restrictive.
  4. B — Pneumothorax.
  5. B — CTPA.
  6. C — mMRC 3.
  7. B — ASD/HPS.
  8. B — Metabolic acidosis.
  9. B — First-line panel.
  10. B — High NPV for HF.

SBAs

  1. B — COPD.
  2. B — Methacholine.
  3. A — DLCO earliest.
  4. B — CXR.
  5. B — CTPA.
  6. B — Heart failure.
  7. B — OHS.
  8. B — IM adrenaline.
  9. B — Unilateral disease.
  10. B — ILD or PV disease.

topics.title: Hemoptysis
topics.html:
[!important]
Haemoptysis is the expectoration of blood from the lower respiratory tract (below the glottis). It ranges from blood-streaked sputum to massive (>200 mL in 24 h) life-threatening haemorrhage. Common causes include bronchiectasis, lung cancer, pneumonia, TB, and PE, but massive haemoptysis carries high mortality and requires urgent airway protection and intervention.

Related: [[Bronchiectasis]], [[Tuberculosis]], [[Lung Cancer]], [[Pneumonia]], [[Pulmonary Embolism]], [[Respiratory Failure]], [[ABG Interpretation]], [[Oxygen Therapy and NIV]], [[Chest X-Ray Approach]], [[Spirometry Interpretation]]

[!tip] FCPS/MRCP pearl: Always distinguish true haemoptysis (from below glottis) from haematemesis (GI bleed) and epistaxis (nasal source). Massive haemoptysis (>200 mL/24 h or >100 mL/episode) = anaesthetic/ITU emergency. Bronchiectasis + TB are the most common causes of massive haemoptysis worldwide. First investigation: CXR (localises in 50–80%); CT + bronchoscopy for definitive diagnosis.

1. Definition

Haemoptysis = coughing up blood from the lower respiratory tract.

Severity grading

SeverityVolumeAction
Non-massive (scant–mild)<30 mL/24 hOutpatient workup if stable
Moderate30–200 mL/24 hAdmit, urgent workup
Massive>200 mL/24 h OR >100 mL/episode OR rapid airway compromiseAnaesthetic/ITU emergency, airway protection, bronchoscopy, embolisation
[!warning] Massive haemoptysis = asphyxiation from blood filling the airways is the usual cause of death (not exsanguination). Priorities are airway protection (intubate, isolate lung with bleeding) and bleeding control (bronchoscopy, embolisation, surgery).

2. Pathophysiology

Source of bleeding

  • High-pressure bronchial arteries (90% of cases) — originate from aorta, supply airways; susceptible to chronic inflammation → neovascularisation, friable vessels
  • Low-pressure pulmonary arteries (5%) — ruptured by PE, infection, vasculitis
  • Capillary bleeding — alveolar haemorrhage (vasculitis, coagulopathy)

Mechanisms

  • Chronic inflammation (bronchiectasis, TB) → neovascularisation → vessel rupture
  • Tumour invasion (lung cancer) → erosion into vessels
  • Cavitary disease (TB, aspergilloma) → vessel erosion
  • Vascular abnormalities (PE, AVM, vasculitis)
  • Coagulopathy + minor lung insult

3. Aetiology

By frequency

CauseNotes
BronchiectasisMost common cause of massive haemoptysis worldwide
Lung cancerSquamous most often; central, cavitating
TB (active or old)Common in endemic areas; Rasmussen aneurysm
PneumoniaEspecially with Klebsiella (currant-jelly sputum)
PEUsually mild; pulmonary infarction
BronchitisCommon benign cause
FungalAspergilloma in pre-existing cavity
VasculitisGPA (Wegener's), MPA, EGPA
AVMHereditary haemorrhagic telangiectasia
CoagulopathyAnticoagulants, thrombocytopenia, DIC, leukaemia
CardiacMitral stenosis (pulmonary hypertension), LV failure
Trauma / iatrogenicBronchoscopy, biopsy, PA catheter, CVC
DrugsAntiplatelets, anticoagulants, cocaine, thrombolytics
Endometriosis (catamenial)Cyclical haemoptysis with menstruation
Idiopathic / cryptogenic5–10%; diagnosis of exclusion

4. Clinical Features

History

  • Volume of blood (estimated by cupfuls, spoonfuls)
  • Frequency (single episode vs recurrent)
  • Preceding symptoms: cough, fever, night sweats, weight loss (cancer, TB)
  • Smoking (cancer, COPD)
  • TB exposure, prior TB, immigration from endemic area
  • Recent immobilisation, surgery, OCP (PE)
  • Aspergillus exposure (cavitary disease)
  • Epistaxis, oral ulceration, haematuria, rash (vasculitis)
  • Menstrual cycle (catamenial)
  • Drugs (anticoagulants, antiplatelets, cocaine)
  • Prior bronchiectasis, CF, lung disease

Examination

  • Vital signs: RR, HR, BP, SpO₂, temperature
  • Volume status: signs of shock (massive bleed)
  • Anaemia: pallor
  • Cachexia, clubbing (cancer, chronic disease)
  • Lymphadenopathy (malignancy, TB)
  • Chest exam: focal crackles, wheeze, dullness, pleural rub
  • Cardiac: murmurs (mitral stenosis), raised JVP
  • ENT: epistaxis, oral cavity
  • Skin: telangiectasia (HHT), rash (vasculitis, SLE)
Differentiating from haematemesis
pHHaemoptysis alkaline, haematemesis acidic
ColourBright red (haemoptysis) vs dark brown (haematemesis)
Mixed withSputum (haemoptysis) vs food (haematemesis)
Preceded byCough (haemoptysis) vs vomiting (haematemesis)
FoamyYes (haemoptysis) — frothy sputum

5. Investigations

First-line

TestRole
CXRLocalises bleeding in 50–80%; masses, consolidation, cavities
FBC, U&E, LFT, clottingAnaemia, coagulopathy, renal (vasculitis)
ABGOxygenation; alveolar haemorrhage ↑A-a gradient
SputumCulture, AFB, cytology
ECGRight heart strain, ischaemia
BNPCardiac cause
UrinalysisVasculitis (haematuria)

Second-line

  • CTPA — PE; CT chest with contrast — most useful for localising bleeding, masses, bronchiectasis, aspergilloma
  • Bronchoscopy (rigid preferred for massive) — direct visualisation, biopsy, therapeutic (laser, electrocautery, iced saline, adrenaline, balloon tamponade)
  • Angiography (bronchial artery) — definitive diagnosis + therapeutic embolisation
  • Echocardiogram — mitral stenosis, pulmonary hypertension
  • Vasculitis screen — ANCA, anti-GBM, ANA, complement
  • Coagulation — coagulation profile, mixing studies

6. Diagnosis — algorithm

⬇ Clinical Pathway
True haemoptysis
↓
Volume?
↓
ITU/anaesthesia; protect airway, isolate bleeding lung, bronchoscopy + embolisation
CXR + CT chest with contrast + labs
↓
CXR/CT findings
↓
Bronchoscopy + biopsy
Sputum AFB/fungal, aspergilloma, tumour
Treat infection, consider embolisation
Antibiotics, treat pneumonia
CTPA, bronchoscopy, ENT exam, vasculitis
↓
Dx confirmed?
↓
Treat cause
Angiography, observe

7. Management

Massive haemoptysis — emergency

  1. Airway — intubation with large-bore ETT (≥8.0 mm); consider selective intubation of non-bleeding lung with patient in lateral decubitus (bleeding side down)
  2. Breathing — high-flow O₂, suction
  3. Circulation — large-bore IV access, fluid resuscitation, cross-match
  4. Localise — CXR / CT / bronchoscopy (rigid preferred for visualisation and intervention)
  5. Bleeding control:
  • Bronchoscopic: iced saline, adrenaline, laser, electrocautery, bronchial blocker (e.g. Fogarty)
  • Bronchial artery embolisation (BAE) — first-line interventional for massive
  • Surgical resection — last resort (lobectomy, pneumonectomy)
  1. Reverse coagulopathy — vitamin K, FFP, PCC, platelets
  2. Treat underlying cause — antibiotics, anti-TB, chemotherapy

Non-massive

  • Admit, monitor SpO₂, IV access
  • CXR + CT chest (or directly CT if high suspicion)
  • Treat underlying cause (infection, inflammation, cancer, PE)
  • Reversal of anticoagulation if appropriate
  • Cough suppression (codeine) — cautious, avoid suppressing productive cough
  • Tranexamic acid — controversial; possible benefit
  • Bronchoscopy for diagnostic and therapeutic

Specific treatment

CauseTreatment
BronchiectasisAntibiotics, postural drainage, BAE for recurrent/massive
Lung cancerResection, radiotherapy (including endobronchial brachytherapy), BAE
TBAnti-TB therapy
AspergillomaSurgical resection if isolated; BAE; itraconazole/voriconazole
PEAnticoagulation
Vasculitis (GPA)Steroids + cyclophosphamide/rituximab
AVMEmbolisation
Mitral stenosisDiuretics, valve intervention
CoagulopathyReverse anticoagulation, replace factors/platelets

8. Complications

  • Asphyxiation (main cause of death in massive)
  • Exsanguination (rare)
  • Aspiration pneumonia (blood to non-bleeding lung)
  • ARDS (from blood + inflammatory response)
  • Airway obstruction, atelectasis
  • Secondary infection
  • Anaemia
  • Death (massive: 30–50% mortality if untreated)

9. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

DomainKey points
DefinitionBlood from lower respiratory tract
Massive>200 mL/24 h or >100 mL/episode
Death in massiveUsually asphyxiation, not exsanguination
Most common cause (massive)Bronchiectasis + TB worldwide
Common causes (mild)Bronchitis, bronchiectasis, cancer, TB, PE
CXRFirst investigation; localises 50–80%
CTBest non-invasive test
BronchoscopyDiagnostic + therapeutic (rigid for massive)
Bronchial artery embolisationFirst-line interventional for massive
SurgeryLast resort
AirwayLateral decubitus (bleeding side down), selective intubation
Distinguish from haematemesispH, colour, food, cough/vomit, frothy
VasculitisConsider in recurrent + extrapulmonary features

10. Common Viva Questions

QuestionExpected answer
Define massive haemoptysis.>200 mL/24 h or >100 mL/episode or rapid airway compromise.
What is the most common cause of massive haemoptysis?Bronchiectasis and TB (worldwide).
What is the most common cause of death in massive haemoptysis?Asphyxiation from blood filling the airways.
How do you distinguish haemoptysis from haematemesis?pH, colour, food, cough/vomit, frothy sputum, melaena.
First-line investigation?CXR.
Best non-invasive investigation?CT chest with contrast.
First-line interventional for massive?Bronchial artery embolisation (BAE).
Position of patient with massive haemoptysis?Lateral decubitus, bleeding side down.
3 drug causes?Anticoagulants, antiplatelets, cocaine, thrombolytics.
3 vasculitis causes?GPA (Wegener's), MPA, EGPA, anti-GBM (Goodpasture).

11. Confusions & Mnemonics

Causes of haemoptysis — "BATTLE CAMP": Bronchiectasis, AVM, TB, Tumour, Lung abscess, Embolism, Coagulopathy, Alveolar haemorrhage, Mitral stenosis, Pneumonia

Massive haemoptysis management — "A-B-C-D-E": Airway (intubate, lateral decubitus), Breathing (O₂), Circulation (IV access, fluid), Diagnose/localise (CXR, CT, bronchoscopy), Embolise/surgery

Distinguish from haematemesis — "Haemoptysis = Coughed, Bright red, Frothy, Alkaline"

12. Local Navigation

  • Chapter MOC: [[../Respiratory MOC|Respiratory MOC]]
  • Related: [[Bronchiectasis]] · [[Tuberculosis]] · [[Lung Cancer]] · [[Pneumonia]] · [[Pulmonary Embolism]] · [[Respiratory Failure]] · [[ABG Interpretation]] · [[Oxygen Therapy and NIV]] · [[Chest X-Ray Approach]] · [[Spirometry Interpretation]] · [[Pleural Effusion]]

13. MCQs (10)

  1. Massive haemoptysis is defined as:

A. >500 mL/24 h

B. >200 mL/24 h or >100 mL/episode

C. >50 mL total

D. Any blood

E. Recurrent

Answer: B — >200 mL/24 h or >100 mL/episode.

  1. Most common cause of massive haemoptysis worldwide:

A. Lung cancer

B. Bronchiectasis (and TB)

C. Pneumonia

D. PE

E. Vasculitis

Answer: B — Bronchiectasis + TB.

  1. Most common cause of death in massive haemoptysis:

A. Exsanguination

B. Asphyxiation from blood in airways

C. Shock

D. PE

E. MI

Answer: B — Asphyxiation, not exsanguination.

  1. Most useful initial investigation:

A. CTPA

B. CXR

C. Bronchoscopy

D. CT

E. ABG

Answer: B — CXR localises in 50–80%.

  1. Best non-invasive imaging for haemoptysis:

A. CXR

B. CT chest with contrast

C. V/Q

D. MRI

E. Ultrasound

Answer: B — CT best non-invasive.

  1. First-line interventional for massive haemoptysis:

A. Surgery

B. Bronchial artery embolisation (BAE)

C. Bronchoscopy only

D. Ligation

E. Radiotherapy

Answer: B — BAE first-line.

  1. Distinguishing feature of haemoptysis vs haematemesis:

A. Dark colour for haemoptysis

B. Bright red, frothy, alkaline, mixed with sputum

C. Coffee-ground

D. Melaena

E. Acidic

Answer: B — Bright red, frothy, alkaline, with sputum.

  1. Position of patient with massive haemoptysis:

A. Supine

B. Lateral decubitus, bleeding side down

C. Prone

D. Sitting upright

E. Trendelenburg

Answer: B — Bleeding side down to protect non-bleeding lung.

  1. Cancer-associated massive haemoptysis management:

A. Conservative only

B. BAE + radiotherapy +/− surgery

C. Antibiotics

D. Steroids

E. Chemo only

Answer: B — Multi-modal.

  1. First step in massive haemoptysis management:

A. Bronchoscopy

B. Airway protection (intubation if needed)

C. CXR

D. Embolisation

E. CT

Answer: B — Airway first.

14. SBA Questions (10)

  1. A 50-year-old with bronchiectasis has 300 mL haemoptysis. First step:

A. CXR

B. Protect airway + IV access + cross-match + prepare for bronchoscopy/BAE

C. Discharge

D. Oral antibiotics

E. Steroids

Answer: B — Resuscitate + secure airway.

  1. A 60-year-old smoker with weight loss and recurrent small-volume haemoptysis. Most likely:

A. Bronchiectasis

B. Lung cancer

C. TB

D. PE

E. Pneumonia

Answer: B — Cancer: smoker + weight loss + haemoptysis.

  1. A 25-year-old with episodic haemoptysis around menstruation. Diagnosis:

A. Endometriosis

B. Catamenial haemoptysis (thoracic endometriosis)

C. Sarcoid

D. TB

E. None

Answer: B — Catamenial = cyclical with menses.

  1. A 45-year-old with haemoptysis, sinusitis, glomerulonephritis, ANCA positive. Diagnosis:

A. Sarcoid

B. Granulomatosis with polyangiitis (GPA)

C. TB

D. Asthma

E. Cancer

Answer: B — GPA.

  1. A 55-year-old with sudden pleuritic pain, mild haemoptysis, A-a gradient, D-dimer ↑. Diagnosis:

A. Pneumonia

B. Pulmonary embolism

C. Asthma

D. TB

E. Cancer

Answer: B — PE with pulmonary infarction.

  1. Haemoptysis with cavitating lesion and air crescent sign:

A. TB

B. Aspergilloma (mycetoma)

C. Cancer

D. Abscess

E. Wegener's

Answer: B — Aspergilloma classic.

  1. First-line treatment of aspergilloma with recurrent haemoptysis:

A. Antifungal only

B. Surgical resection if isolated; BAE for inoperable

C. Steroids

D. Observation

E. Radiotherapy

Answer: B — Surgery or BAE.

  1. Recurrent haemoptysis in a known bronchiectasis patient despite antibiotics:

A. Lobe resection

B. BAE first; surgery if recurrent

C. Transplant

D. Steroids

E. Anticoagulation

Answer: B — BAE first.

  1. Rigid vs flexible bronchoscopy in massive haemoptysis:

A. Flexible preferred

B. Rigid preferred (better airway control + suction + intervention)

C. Both equal

D. Neither

E. CT only

Answer: B — Rigid bronchoscopy in massive.

  1. Haemoptysis in pregnancy - first investigation:

A. CTPA

B. CXR; if needed, proceed cautiously with imaging

C. Bronchoscopy

D. MRI

E. No investigation

Answer: B — CXR first; minimise radiation if pregnant.

15. Flashcards

  • Q: Massive haemoptysis definition?

A: >200 mL/24 h or >100 mL/episode.

  • Q: Most common cause of massive haemoptysis?

A: Bronchiectasis (and TB).

  • Q: Most common cause of death in massive haemoptysis?

A: Asphyxiation from blood in airways.

  • Q: Best initial test?

A: CXR.

  • Q: Best non-invasive test?

A: CT chest with contrast.

  • Q: First-line interventional?

A: Bronchial artery embolisation (BAE).

  • Q: Distinguishing haemoptysis from haematemesis?

A: Haemoptysis: bright red, frothy, alkaline, mixed with sputum, preceded by cough.

  • Q: Position of patient?

A: Lateral decubitus, bleeding side down.

  • Q: What is aspergilloma?

A: Fungal ball in pre-existing cavity (TB, sarcoid) with air crescent sign.

  • Q: Catamenial haemoptysis?

A: Cyclical haemoptysis with menstruation (thoracic endometriosis).

16. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. B — >200 mL/24 h.
  2. B — Bronchiectasis.
  3. B — Asphyxiation.
  4. B — CXR.
  5. B — CT with contrast.
  6. B — BAE.
  7. B — Bright red, frothy, alkaline, sputum.
  8. B — Lateral decubitus, bleeding side down.
  9. B — Multi-modal.
  10. B — Airway first.

SBAs

  1. B — Resuscitate.
  2. B — Lung cancer.
  3. B — Catamenial.
  4. B — GPA.
  5. B — PE.
  6. B — Aspergilloma.
  7. B — Surgery or BAE.
  8. B — BAE first.
  9. B — Rigid bronchoscopy.
  10. B — CXR first.

topics.hub_name: Upper Airway and Sleep-Related Breathing Disorders
topics.title: Foreign Body Aspiration In Adults
topics.html:
[!important]
Foreign body (FB) aspiration in adults is often subtle or missed. Common FBs: food (meat, bones), teeth, dental instruments. Common in: elderly, neurological disease (stroke, dementia), alcohol intoxication, dental procedures. Bronchoscopy (rigid preferred) is diagnostic and therapeutic.

Related: [[Upper airway obstruction and stridor]], [[Post-obstructive pneumonia]], [[Aspiration pneumonia]], [[Pneumonia]]

[!tip] FCPS/MRCP pearl: Sudden cough/choking + unilateral wheeze + localised crackles = foreign body until proven otherwise → rigid bronchoscopy. In elderly, neurological disease, alcoholics, dental procedures = high suspicion. Recurrent post-obstructive pneumonia in same lobe = consider FB.

1. Definition

Foreign body (FB) aspiration = inhalation of foreign material into the airways (larynx, trachea, bronchi).

2. Aetiology

Common objects

ObjectNotes
Food (meat, peanuts, bones, fish)Most common in adults
Teeth, dental restorationsDental procedures, trauma
Dental instrumentsDuring procedures
Coins (children)Rare in adults
Bones (chicken, fish)Common
Vomitus (aspiration)Common in alcoholics
PillsElderly
Foreign bodies (e.g. pins, teeth)Trauma, drug smuggling

Risk factors

  • Elderly (impaired swallow, dentition)
  • Neurological disease (stroke, dementia, Parkinson's, MS, MG, GBS)
  • Alcohol intoxication (↓consciousness, ↓gag reflex)
  • Sedation (anaesthesia, drugs)
  • Dental procedures
  • Tracheostomy / intubation
  • Achalasia, oesophageal stricture
  • Psychiatric (pica, deliberate)

3. Pathophysiology

Distribution

  • Right main bronchus more often (wider, more vertical) in adults
  • Depends on position at aspiration (upright → lower lobes; supine → upper lobes)
  • Common locations: right lower lobe, right middle lobe, left lower lobe

Consequences

  • Airway obstruction (partial/complete)
  • Post-obstructive pneumonia (recurrent, same lobe)
  • Atelectasis (distal collapse)
  • Lung abscess (chronic FB)
  • Bronchiectasis (chronic)
  • Pneumothorax, pneumomediastinum (rare)
  • Death (massive obstruction, complete laryngeal)

4. Clinical Features

Acute

  • Sudden cough, choking
  • Stridor (laryngeal)
  • Wheeze (unilateral, focal)
  • Dyspnoea
  • Cyanosis
  • Loss of consciousness (severe)
  • Cardiac arrest (massive)

Subacute / chronic

  • Persistent cough
  • Recurrent pneumonia (same lobe)
  • Haemoptysis
  • Wheeze, stridor
  • Lung abscess (fever, foul sputum)
  • Non-resolving pneumonia

Signs

  • Focal wheeze (monophonic, fixed)
  • ↓Breath sounds distally
  • Crackles (post-obstructive)
  • Hyperresonance (ball-valve effect)
  • Stridor (laryngeal)

5. Diagnosis

Investigations

TestPurpose
CXR (PA + lateral, inspiration + expiration)Radiopaque FB, air-trapping, atelectasis, post-obstructive pneumonia
CT chestMore sensitive for radiolucent FB
BronchoscopyGold standard — diagnostic + therapeutic
Spirometry (flow-volume loop)Variable intrathoracic obstruction
ABGSeverity

CXR findings

  • Radiopaque FB: direct visualisation
  • Radiolucent FB: indirect signs
  • Air-trapping (hyperinflation distal to ball-valve)
  • Atelectasis (complete obstruction)
  • Post-obstructive pneumonia (consolidation)
  • Mediastinal shift (with atelectasis)
  • Normal CXR does not exclude FB
  • Inspiratory + expiratory films: look for air-trapping

6. Management

Emergency

  • A–B–C assessment
  • O₂ (target SpO₂ 94–98%)
  • Heimlich manoeuvre (complete laryngeal obstruction, conscious)
  • Back blows + abdominal thrusts (choking)
  • Direct laryngoscopy + Magill forceps (laryngeal FB)
  • Cricothyroidotomy/tracheostomy (if intubation fails)
  • Heimlich is contraindicated if patient can still cough/cough effectively (let them cough)

Bronchoscopy

  • Rigid bronchoscopy (gold standard for FB removal in adults)
  • Better airway control
  • Larger forceps
  • General anaesthesia
  • Flexible bronchoscopy (alternative for small, peripheral FB)

Supportive

  • O₂, ventilation as needed
  • Antibiotics if post-obstructive pneumonia (after removal)
  • Physiotherapy
  • Bronchodilators (wheeze)

Prevention

  • Dental care (especially elderly)
  • Avoid talking while eating (supine)
  • Aspiration precautions (stroke, dementia)
  • Choking first aid education

7. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

DomainKey points
DefinitionInhaled foreign material
Risk factorsElderly, neurological, alcohol, dental
DistributionRight main bronchus more common
SymptomsSudden cough, choking, unilateral wheeze
Best imagingCXR (inspiration + expiration) + CT
TreatmentRigid bronchoscopy (gold standard)
EmergencyHeimlich + laryngoscopy + intubation/tracheostomy
Recurrent pneumoniaSame lobe = consider FB

8. MCQs (10)

  1. The most common location of foreign body aspiration in adults:

A. Right main bronchus

B. Left main bronchus

C. Trachea

D. None

E. Larynx

Answer: A — Right (wider, more vertical).

  1. The most common foreign body in adults:

A. Teeth

B. Food (meat, bones, peanuts)

C. Coins

D. Pins

E. None

Answer: B — Food.

  1. The most useful investigation for suspected FB:

A. CXR

B. CT chest + bronchoscopy

C. Spirogram

D. ABG

E. None

Answer: B — CT + bronchoscopy.

  1. The first-line treatment of foreign body aspiration:

A. Steroids

B. Bronchoscopy (rigid preferred)

C. Antibiotics

D. Wait

Answer: B — Rigid bronchoscopy.

  1. CXR in FB may show:

A. Air-trapping + atelectasis + consolidation

B. Normal

C. Mass

D. None

E. Cavitation

Answer: A — Indirect signs.

  1. A normal CXR excludes FB:

A. Yes

B. No (radiolucent FB can be missed)

C. Always

D. None

E. Sometimes

Answer: B — No.

  1. The emergency treatment of complete laryngeal obstruction in conscious patient:

A. Cricothyroidotomy

B. Heimlich + back blows

C. Intubation

D. Wait

E. None

Answer: B — Heimlich.

  1. The most common cause of recurrent lobar pneumonia:

A. Asthma

B. Foreign body (or endobronchial tumour)

C. COPD

D. None

E. Pneumonia

Answer: B — FB or tumour.

  1. The risk factor most associated with FB aspiration in adults:

A. Asthma

B. Elderly with neurological disease (stroke, dementia)

C. COPD

D. None

E. Pneumonia

Answer: B — Elderly + neurological.

  1. The flow-volume loop in FB (intrabronchial) shows:

A. Variable intrathoracic obstruction (flattened expiratory limb)

B. Normal

C. Fixed

D. None

E. Variable extrathoracic

Answer: A — Variable intrathoracic.

9. SBA Questions (10)

  1. A 70-year-old with dementia has sudden cough, unilateral wheeze, post-obstructive pneumonia. Most likely:

A. Asthma

B. Foreign body aspiration

C. COPD

D. None

E. PE

Answer: B — FB.

  1. The best first investigation for suspected FB in adult:

A. CXR (inspiration + expiration) + CT

B. ABG

C. Spirogram

D. None

E. Echo

Answer: A — CXR + CT.

  1. The treatment of choice for FB in adult:

A. Wait

B. Rigid bronchoscopy under GA

C. Steroids

D. None

E. Antibiotics

Answer: B — Rigid bronchoscopy.

  1. A 60-year-old with sudden complete laryngeal obstruction. Emergency Rx:

A. Wait

B. Heimlich +/− cricothyroidotomy if fails

C. Steroids

D. None

E. Antibiotics

Answer: B — Heimlich.

  1. The most common lung region for FB aspiration in upright adults:

A. Right lower lobe (posterior basal segment)

B. RUL

C. LUL

D. None

E. Trachea

Answer: A — RLL.

  1. The most common lung region for FB in supine adults:

A. RLL

B. RUL (posterior segment) / superior segment of lower lobes

C. LUL

D. None

E. Lingula

Answer: B — Posterior upper / superior lower.

  1. FB aspiration in a 50-year-old after dental procedure. Most likely:

A. Bone

B. Tooth or dental instrument

C. Food

D. None

E. Coin

Answer: B — Dental.

  1. The most common cause of chronic lung abscess in adult:

A. Pneumonia

B. Foreign body (anaerobic superinfection)

C. TB

D. None

E. PE

Answer: B — FB (anaerobic).

  1. The first step in management of stable FB aspiration:

A. Bronchoscopy (urgent)

B. Bronchoscopy (semi-urgent, within 24 h)

C. Steroids

D. Wait

E. None

Answer: B — Semi-urgent (24 h).

  1. The most useful prevention of FB aspiration in elderly:

A. Diet

B. Dental care + aspiration precautions + avoid alcohol

C. Exercise

D. None

E. Steroids

Answer: B — Dental care + precautions.

10. Flashcards

  • Q: Most common FB location?

A: Right main bronchus.

  • Q: Most common FB in adults?

A: Food (meat, bones).

  • Q: Risk factors?

A: Elderly, neurological, alcohol, dental.

  • Q: Best investigation?

A: CXR + CT + bronchoscopy.

  • Q: Best treatment?

A: Rigid bronchoscopy (GA).

  • Q: Emergency airway obstruction Rx?

A: Heimlich + back blows.

  • Q: Recurrent post-obstructive pneumonia?

A: Consider FB or tumour.

  • Q: CXR normal?

A: Doesn't exclude FB (radiolucent).

  • Q: Right lower lobe (upright)?

A: Posterior basal.

  • Q: Posterior upper lobe (supine)?

A: Supine aspiration.

11. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. A 2. B 3. B 4. B 5. A 6. B 7. B 8. B 9. B 10. A

SBAs

  1. B 2. A 3. B 4. B 5. A 6. B 7. B 8. B 9. B 10. B

12. Summary

FB aspiration in adults: often subtle. Risk factors: elderly, neurological, alcohol, dental. Most common object: food. Most common location: right main bronchus. Best imaging: CXR (inspiration + expiration) + CT. Best treatment: rigid bronchoscopy (GA). Emergency: Heimlich + laryngoscopy. Recurrent post-obstructive pneumonia = consider FB.

13. Local Navigation

  • Parent Heading: [[../Upper Airway and Sleep-Related Breathing Disorders|Upper Airway and Sleep-Related Breathing Disorders]]
  • Parent Topic Group: [[../Upper Airway and Sleep-Related Breathing Disorders/Upper airway disorders|Upper airway disorders]]
  • Chapter Map: [[../Davidson Chapter 17 - Respiratory Medicine Hierarchy|Respiratory Medicine Hierarchy]]
  • Chapter MOC: [[../Respiratory MOC|Respiratory MOC]]
  • Related: [[Upper airway obstruction and stridor]] · [[Post-obstructive pneumonia]] · [[Aspiration pneumonia]]

topics.hub_name: Upper Airway and Sleep-Related Breathing Disorders
topics.title: Obesity Hypoventilation Syndrome
topics.html:
Parent: [[Upper Airway and Sleep-Related Breathing Disorders/Sleep-related breathing disorders|Sleep-related breathing disorders]]

Heading: [[Upper Airway and Sleep-Related Breathing Disorders]]

Related: [[Respiratory MOC]], [[Obstructive sleep apnea]], [[Respiratory Failure and Ventilatory Support/Type 2 respiratory failure|Type 2 respiratory failure]], [[Oxygen Therapy and NIV]], [[ABG Interpretation]]

[!important]
Obesity hypoventilation syndrome (OHS) is defined by obesity plus chronic daytime alveolar hypoventilation causing awake hypercapnia, after excluding other major causes of hypoventilation.

1. Definition

OHS is classically defined by:

  • BMI ≥30 kg/m²
  • Daytime PaCO2 >45 mmHg (6.0 kPa)
  • Sleep-disordered breathing is usually present
  • No alternative explanation such as major neuromuscular, chest wall, or severe intrinsic lung disease alone

2. Anatomy

Relevant anatomical factors

  • Increased chest wall and abdominal mass reduce respiratory system compliance
  • Diaphragmatic excursion is mechanically limited by central obesity
  • Upper airway narrowing often coexists, especially with OSA

3. Physiology

Normal physiology

Adequate ventilation requires:

  • effective respiratory drive
  • sufficient respiratory muscle performance
  • compliant chest wall-lung system
  • patent upper airway

Physiological disturbances in OHS

  • Increased work of breathing due to obesity
  • Reduced chest wall compliance
  • Lower functional residual capacity
  • Ventilation-perfusion disturbance in dependent lung regions
  • Blunted ventilatory response to CO2 in some patients
  • Frequent coexistence of OSA leading to recurrent nocturnal hypoventilation

4. Classification

Practical clinical classification

  • OHS with coexisting OSA
  • OHS without significant OSA
  • Stable chronic OHS
  • Acute decompensated OHS with acute-on-chronic hypercapnic respiratory failure

5. Etiology / Risk Factors

  • Severe obesity
  • Central adiposity
  • Coexisting OSA
  • Sedatives / alcohol
  • Reduced mobility
  • Chronic sleep-disordered breathing
  • Obesity-related cardiometabolic disease

6. Pathophysiology

  1. Obesity increases mechanical load on the respiratory system
  2. Tidal breathing becomes less efficient
  3. Nocturnal hypoventilation develops, often with OSA overlap
  4. Over time, CO2 retention persists into wakefulness
  5. Chronic hypoxemia/hypercapnia may cause pulmonary hypertension and right heart strain

7. Clinical Features

Symptoms

  • Daytime somnolence
  • Loud snoring
  • Morning headache
  • Fatigue
  • Reduced exercise tolerance
  • Dyspnea
  • Poor concentration

Signs

  • Obesity
  • Hypoxemia
  • Hypercapnic features in advanced cases
  • Peripheral edema
  • Raised JVP / signs of cor pulmonale in severe disease
  • Cyanosis in advanced decompensation

8. Investigations

1. ABG

Most important confirming test:

  • Daytime hypercapnia
  • Hypoxemia may coexist
  • HCO3- may be elevated in chronic compensation

2. Sleep study

  • Evaluates coexisting OSA and nocturnal hypoventilation

3. Serum bicarbonate

  • Elevated bicarbonate may suggest chronic CO2 retention
  • Not diagnostic alone

4. Pulse oximetry / overnight oximetry

  • Shows nocturnal desaturation but does not fully define the syndrome

5. CXR / imaging

  • May exclude alternative cardiopulmonary pathology
  • Can show cardiomegaly or pulmonary vascular prominence in advanced disease

6. Cardiac assessment

  • ECG / echocardiography if pulmonary hypertension or right heart strain suspected

9. Interpretation Frameworks

A. ABG interpretation

Typical chronic OHS pattern:

  • PaCO2 elevated while awake
  • PaO2 low or low-normal
  • bicarbonate elevated if chronic compensation is present

B. Oxygen therapy cautions

  • OHS is a hypercapnic-prone state
  • Excess uncontrolled oxygen can worsen CO2 retention in some patients
  • Use oxygen carefully with monitoring, especially during acute decompensation

C. Sleep-disordered breathing overlap

  • OHS often coexists with OSA
  • Isolated OSA may have normal daytime PaCO2; awake hypercapnia favors OHS

D. Imaging clues

  • Imaging is supportive rather than diagnostic
  • Use imaging mainly to exclude competing pathology or complications

10. Diagnosis

Diagnosis requires all of the following:

  • obesity
  • chronic daytime hypercapnia
  • exclusion of other major causes of alveolar hypoventilation
  • supportive sleep-disordered breathing assessment in most cases

11. Differential Diagnosis

  • Isolated OSA without daytime hypercapnia
  • COPD with chronic hypercapnia
  • Neuromuscular respiratory weakness
  • Kyphoscoliosis / chest wall disease
  • Hypothyroidism-related hypoventilation
  • Drug-induced hypoventilation

12. Management

General measures

  • Weight reduction is central
  • Treat comorbid diabetes, hypertension, and heart failure risk
  • Avoid sedatives and excess alcohol

Ventilatory support

  • CPAP if OSA is dominant and hypercapnia is mild/overlap-driven
  • NIV/BiPAP often required when significant hypoventilation/hypercapnia persists

Oxygen

  • Give only when indicated
  • Monitor ABG because uncontrolled oxygen can aggravate hypercapnia

Acute decompensation

  • Treat as acute-on-chronic hypercapnic respiratory failure
  • Controlled oxygen, ABG monitoring, NIV where appropriate, and identify precipitating factors such as infection or sedative exposure

13. Complications

  • Pulmonary hypertension
  • Cor pulmonale
  • Polycythemia in chronic hypoxemia
  • Recurrent hospital admission
  • Severe daytime disability
  • Increased cardiovascular risk

14. Red Flags / Emergencies

  • Drowsiness / confusion from hypercapnia
  • Acute-on-chronic respiratory acidosis
  • Cor pulmonale / fluid overload
  • Severe hypoxemia or decompensation during infection

15. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Points

  • OHS = obesity + daytime hypercapnia after exclusion of other causes
  • Many patients also have OSA
  • Persistent awake hypercapnia distinguishes OHS from uncomplicated OSA
  • Oxygen must be used cautiously because of CO2 retention risk
  • NIV is often more important than oxygen alone

16. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

  • Do not label every obese snorer as OHS
  • OHS is not diagnosed by nocturnal desaturation alone
  • Do not miss chronic hypercapnia on ABG
  • Do not give uncontrolled high-flow oxygen without reassessment in hypercapnic-prone patients

17. One-Page Summary

  • OHS = obesity + chronic daytime alveolar hypoventilation
  • Key ABG clue: awake PaCO2 >45 mmHg
  • Frequently overlaps with OSA
  • Main issues: sleep-disordered breathing, chronic hypercapnia, pulmonary hypertension/cor pulmonale risk
  • Management: weight reduction, CPAP/NIV depending on physiology, cautious oxygen, monitor ABG

18. Revision Prompts

  • Define OHS precisely
  • How do you differentiate OHS from isolated OSA?
  • Why can oxygen worsen hypercapnia in OHS?
  • When is NIV especially useful in OHS?
  • List complications of untreated OHS

19. MCQs

  1. OHS is best defined by obesity plus:
  • A. Isolated nocturnal snoring
  • B. Daytime hypercapnia
  • C. Hemoptysis
  • D. Pleural effusion
  • Answer: B
  1. A key ABG feature of OHS is:
  • A. Very low PaCO2
  • B. Normal PaCO2 in all cases
  • C. PaCO2 >45 mmHg while awake
  • D. Isolated metabolic alkalosis only
  • Answer: C
  1. OHS commonly overlaps with:
  • A. Pneumothorax
  • B. OSA
  • C. Sarcoidosis
  • D. Bronchiectasis
  • Answer: B
  1. In OHS, uncontrolled oxygen may:
  • A. Always cure hypoventilation
  • B. Worsen CO2 retention
  • C. Eliminate need for ventilation
  • D. Prevent obesity
  • Answer: B
  1. A major chronic complication of untreated OHS is:
  • A. Left pneumothorax
  • B. Cor pulmonale
  • C. Tension gastrothorax
  • D. Hemothorax
  • Answer: B
  1. A patient with obesity, snoring, and normal daytime PaCO2 most likely has:
  • A. Proven OHS
  • B. Possible OSA rather than confirmed OHS
  • C. Definite PE
  • D. ILD
  • Answer: B
  1. Central long-term management of OHS includes:
  • A. Weight reduction
  • B. Routine antibiotics
  • C. Anticoagulation for all
  • D. Bronchoscopy for all
  • Answer: A
  1. Which ventilatory strategy is often required in significant persistent hypercapnia?
  • A. Nebulized salbutamol only
  • B. NIV/BiPAP
  • C. Chest drain
  • D. Pleurodesis
  • Answer: B
  1. Elevated serum bicarbonate in OHS often reflects:
  • A. Acute hemorrhage
  • B. Chronic CO2 retention with renal compensation
  • C. Simple viral URTI
  • D. Lobar collapse alone
  • Answer: B
  1. A diagnostic requirement for OHS is exclusion of:
  • A. Any snoring history
  • B. Other causes of alveolar hypoventilation
  • C. All cardiac disease
  • D. All lung disease of any degree
  • Answer: B

20. SBA Questions

  1. A 44-year-old woman with BMI 46 kg/m² has daytime somnolence and ABG shows PaCO2 52 mmHg. Most likely diagnosis?

Answer: Obesity hypoventilation syndrome

  1. Best test to confirm chronic daytime hypoventilation in suspected OHS?

Answer: Arterial blood gas

  1. An obese patient with sleep-disordered breathing is drowsy and hypercapnic. Best ventilatory support consideration if persistent?

Answer: NIV/BiPAP

  1. What best distinguishes OHS from simple OSA?

Answer: Persistent awake hypercapnia

  1. Why should oxygen be used carefully in OHS?

Answer: It may worsen CO2 retention in hypercapnic-prone patients

  1. A chronic complication of untreated OHS is:

Answer: Pulmonary hypertension

  1. A raised serum bicarbonate in an obese sleepy patient should make you think of:

Answer: Chronic CO2 retention

  1. Long-term disease-modifying measure in OHS?

Answer: Weight reduction

  1. Which overlap is most common in OHS?

Answer: OSA overlap

  1. During acute infection with worsening hypercapnia in OHS, immediate reassessment should include:

Answer: Controlled oxygen with repeat ABG monitoring

21. Flashcards

  • Q: Core diagnostic triad of OHS?

A: Obesity, daytime hypercapnia, and exclusion of other major causes of hypoventilation.

  • Q: Most important ABG clue for OHS?

A: Awake PaCO2 >45 mmHg.

  • Q: Common overlap disorder in OHS?

A: OSA.

  • Q: Why must oxygen be given carefully in OHS?

A: It can worsen CO2 retention.

  • Q: Important ventilatory support modality in persistent hypercapnic OHS?

A: NIV/BiPAP.

22. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. B — OHS.
  2. B — Sleep study + ABG.
  3. B — Daytime hypercapnia.
  4. B — PAP (CPAP or BiPAP).
  5. B — Weight loss + PAP.
  6. B — OHS.
  7. B — OHS.
  8. B — OSA.
  9. B — OHS.
  10. B — Daytime PaCO₂ >6 kPa.

SBAs

  1. B — Sleep study + ABG.
  2. B — NIV + weight loss.
  3. B — Weight loss.
  4. B — BiPAP.
  5. B — 1 year.
  6. B — Obesity + hypoventilation.
  7. B — First-line PAP.
  8. B — Bariatric surgery.
  9. B — Low threshold.
  10. B — Combined.

topics.title: Obstructive Sleep Apnea
topics.html:
Parent: [[Upper Airway and Sleep-Related Breathing Disorders/Sleep-related breathing disorders|Sleep-related breathing disorders]]

Heading: [[Upper Airway and Sleep-Related Breathing Disorders]]

Related: [[Respiratory MOC]], [[Obesity hypoventilation syndrome]], [[Respiratory Failure and Ventilatory Support/Type 2 respiratory failure|Type 2 respiratory failure]], [[Oxygen Therapy and NIV]], [[ABG Interpretation]]

[!important]
Obstructive sleep apnea (OSA) is recurrent partial or complete upper-airway collapse during sleep, causing intermittent hypoxemia, sleep fragmentation, loud snoring, and excessive daytime sleepiness.

1. Definition

OSA is characterized by repetitive episodes of:

  • Apnea = cessation of airflow for at least 10 seconds despite ongoing respiratory effort
  • Hypopnea = reduction in airflow with oxygen desaturation and/or arousal

2. Anatomy

Upper airway structures relevant to OSA

  • Nasopharynx, oropharynx, soft palate, uvula, tongue base, and hypopharynx
  • The pharynx is a collapsible muscular tube without rigid cartilaginous support
  • Airway caliber is influenced by:
  • soft-tissue bulk
  • mandibular size and position
  • tonsillar tissue
  • neck circumference
  • neuromuscular tone of pharyngeal dilator muscles

Anatomical risk factors

  • Obesity with parapharyngeal fat deposition
  • Retrognathia / micrognathia
  • Macroglossia
  • Enlarged tonsils / adenoids
  • Nasal obstruction

3. Physiology

Normal sleep-related airway physiology

  • During sleep, especially REM sleep, pharyngeal dilator muscle tone falls
  • In healthy people the airway remains patent despite reduced tone

Pathophysiology in OSA

  • Narrow pharyngeal airway + reduced dilator tone during sleep
  • Negative inspiratory pressure further promotes airway collapse
  • Recurrent obstruction causes:
  • intermittent hypoxemia
  • hypercapnia in some episodes
  • repeated arousals
  • sympathetic activation
  • sleep fragmentation

Physiological consequences

  • Daytime somnolence from fragmented sleep
  • Systemic hypertension from sympathetic surges
  • Pulmonary hypertension / cor pulmonale in advanced disease or overlap states
  • Increased cardiovascular and cerebrovascular risk

4. Classification

By sleep-study severity using apnea-hypopnea index (AHI)

  • Mild: 5-14 events/hour
  • Moderate: 15-29 events/hour
  • Severe: 30 or more events/hour

By clinical context

  • Simple OSA
  • OSA with obesity hypoventilation overlap
  • OSA with chronic lung disease / overlap syndrome

5. Etiology / Risk Factors

  • Obesity
  • Male sex
  • Increasing age
  • Family history
  • Craniofacial narrowing
  • Enlarged tonsils
  • Alcohol or sedative use
  • Hypothyroidism
  • Acromegaly
  • Nasal obstruction
  • Supine sleep posture

6. Pathophysiology

Stepwise mechanism:

  1. Sleep reduces pharyngeal muscle tone
  2. A narrowed upper airway collapses during inspiration
  3. Airflow stops despite chest/abdominal effort
  4. Oxygen saturation falls and CO2 may rise transiently
  5. Brief arousal restores tone and airflow
  6. Cycles repeat many times nightly

7. Clinical Features

Symptoms

  • Loud habitual snoring
  • Witnessed apneas
  • Choking/gasping during sleep
  • Non-refreshing sleep
  • Excessive daytime sleepiness
  • Morning headache
  • Poor concentration / irritability
  • Reduced libido
  • Nocturia

Signs

  • Obesity
  • Large neck circumference
  • Crowded oropharynx
  • Hypertension
  • Features of metabolic syndrome
  • In severe/late disease: signs of pulmonary hypertension or right heart failure

8. Investigations

1. Sleep study

  • Polysomnography is the gold standard
  • Records airflow, respiratory effort, oxygen saturation, sleep stage, and arousals

2. Overnight oximetry

  • Useful screening tool
  • Recurrent nocturnal desaturation suggests sleep-disordered breathing
  • Less definitive than full polysomnography

3. ABG

  • Usually normal in uncomplicated OSA while awake
  • Consider if severe obesity, daytime somnolence with chronic hypercapnia, or suspected obesity hypoventilation syndrome

4. Clinical assessment

  • BMI, neck circumference, blood pressure
  • Epworth Sleepiness Scale
  • Airway examination

5. Comorbidity evaluation

  • ECG, echocardiography if pulmonary hypertension/cardiac disease suspected
  • Thyroid function if clinically indicated

9. Interpretation Frameworks

A. ABG interpretation

  • Normal daytime ABG does not exclude OSA
  • Persistent daytime hypercapnia suggests obesity hypoventilation syndrome rather than simple isolated OSA

B. Oxygen logic

  • Nocturnal oxygen desaturation in OSA is due to repeated airway obstruction
  • Oxygen alone is not definitive treatment because it does not splint the airway open
  • CPAP treats the mechanism by preventing collapse

C. Imaging clues

  • Imaging is not diagnostic in most cases
  • Lateral airway narrowing, obesity-related soft tissue crowding, or craniofacial abnormalities may be contributory findings

10. Diagnosis

Diagnosis is based on:

  • compatible symptoms/signs
  • and objective demonstration of obstructive sleep-disordered breathing on sleep study

11. Differential Diagnosis

  • Simple snoring without apnea
  • Obesity hypoventilation syndrome
  • Central sleep apnea
  • Hypothyroidism-related somnolence
  • Sedative/alcohol-related sleepiness
  • Narcolepsy
  • Depression / poor sleep hygiene

12. Management

General measures

  • Weight reduction
  • Avoid alcohol and sedatives at night
  • Sleep-position advice where relevant
  • Treat nasal obstruction if present

Specific therapy

Continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP)

  • First-line for many symptomatic moderate-severe patients
  • Splints the upper airway open during sleep
  • Improves symptoms, sleep quality, and blood pressure control in many patients

Mandibular advancement device

  • Option in mild-moderate disease or CPAP intolerance

Surgery

  • Selected anatomical cases only
  • Examples: tonsil surgery, upper airway surgery, maxillofacial intervention in select patients

Management of comorbidity

  • Treat hypertension, diabetes, obesity, and cardiovascular risk factors
  • Evaluate for overlap with obesity hypoventilation syndrome

13. Complications

  • Resistant hypertension
  • Arrhythmia
  • Ischemic heart disease
  • Stroke risk increase
  • Pulmonary hypertension
  • Road traffic/work accidents due to sleepiness
  • Poor quality of life

14. Red Flags / Emergencies

  • Severe daytime somnolence impairing driving or machinery operation
  • Suspected obesity hypoventilation with daytime hypercapnia
  • Pulmonary hypertension or cor pulmonale
  • Marked nocturnal desaturation with cardiorespiratory instability

15. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Points

  • OSA = upper airway obstruction during sleep with preserved respiratory effort
  • Severity is commonly graded by AHI
  • Gold standard diagnosis = polysomnography
  • CPAP is a key treatment because it corrects the mechanism
  • Daytime hypercapnia suggests OHS, not isolated simple OSA

16. Common Confusions / Exam Traps

  • Do not confuse OSA with central sleep apnea
  • Do not assume oxygen alone replaces CPAP
  • Do not miss the overlap with obesity hypoventilation syndrome
  • Snoring alone is not enough for diagnosis

17. One-Page Summary

  • OSA is repetitive upper-airway collapse during sleep
  • Typical clues: loud snoring, witnessed apneas, daytime sleepiness, obesity, hypertension
  • Gold standard: polysomnography
  • Severity: mild 5-14, moderate 15-29, severe ≥30 events/hour
  • Treatment: weight loss, avoid sedatives/alcohol, CPAP, oral appliances in selected cases
  • Think of OHS if there is daytime hypercapnia

18. Revision Prompts

  • Define OSA and hypopnea
  • Why is the upper airway vulnerable during sleep?
  • How does OSA differ from OHS?
  • Why is CPAP superior to oxygen alone in typical OSA?
  • List cardiovascular complications of OSA

19. MCQs

  1. The key pathological event in obstructive sleep apnea is:
  • A. Reduced medullary respiratory drive
  • B. Recurrent upper airway collapse during sleep
  • C. Diffusion defect at alveolar membrane
  • D. Loss of surfactant
  • Answer: B
  1. The gold standard test for OSA is:
  • A. CXR
  • B. Spirometry
  • C. Polysomnography
  • D. ABG alone
  • Answer: C
  1. Severe OSA is usually defined by AHI:
  • A. 1-4/hour
  • B. 5-14/hour
  • C. 15-29/hour
  • D. ≥30/hour
  • Answer: D
  1. A common symptom of OSA is:
  • A. Hemoptysis
  • B. Loud snoring
  • C. Clubbing
  • D. Pleuritic chest pain
  • Answer: B
  1. First-line mechanism-correcting treatment in many symptomatic cases is:
  • A. Oxygen by nasal cannula only
  • B. CPAP
  • C. Oral steroids
  • D. Loop diuretic
  • Answer: B
  1. Daytime hypercapnia in an obese patient with sleep-disordered breathing suggests:
  • A. Pneumothorax
  • B. OHS
  • C. Isolated mild OSA only
  • D. Pulmonary embolism
  • Answer: B
  1. A recognized risk factor for OSA is:
  • A. Low BMI
  • B. Obesity
  • C. Neutropenia
  • D. Hyperthyroidism only
  • Answer: B
  1. OSA contributes to all except:
  • A. Hypertension
  • B. Daytime somnolence
  • C. Pulmonary embolism as direct defining feature
  • D. Road traffic accidents
  • Answer: C
  1. In OSA, respiratory effort during obstructive episodes is usually:
  • A. Absent
  • B. Preserved
  • C. Irrelevant
  • D. Always paradoxically lost completely
  • Answer: B
  1. Snoring alone without apneas means:
  • A. Definite OSA
  • B. Definite OHS
  • C. Further assessment is needed
  • D. Definite central apnea
  • Answer: C

20. SBA Questions

  1. A 52-year-old obese man snores loudly, has witnessed apneas, and falls asleep while watching TV. Best investigation?

Answer: Polysomnography

  1. A patient with OSA asks why oxygen alone is insufficient. Best explanation?

Answer: Oxygen does not prevent upper-airway collapse; CPAP splints the airway open.

  1. A 48-year-old woman with BMI 42 kg/m² has sleepiness and elevated PaCO2 while awake. Most likely associated diagnosis?

Answer: Obesity hypoventilation syndrome

  1. Most important modifiable risk factor for OSA?

Answer: Obesity

  1. An AHI of 22/hour indicates:

Answer: Moderate OSA

  1. A crowded oropharynx and large neck circumference in a sleepy snorer suggest:

Answer: Obstructive sleep apnea

  1. Best first-line treatment for symptomatic severe OSA?

Answer: CPAP

  1. A sleepy patient with snoring is a bus driver. Most urgent counseling point?

Answer: Driving risk due to daytime somnolence

  1. Repeated sympathetic surges in OSA increase risk of:

Answer: Systemic hypertension

  1. Which finding would most strongly push you to think beyond isolated OSA?

Answer: Persistent daytime hypercapnia

21. Flashcards

  • Q: What is the core mechanism of OSA?

A: Recurrent upper-airway collapse during sleep despite ongoing respiratory effort.

  • Q: Gold standard test for OSA?

A: Polysomnography.

  • Q: Severe OSA AHI threshold?

A: ≥30 events/hour.

  • Q: First-line mechanism-based treatment?

A: CPAP.

  • Q: What daytime ABG feature suggests OHS rather than isolated OSA?

A: Persistent daytime hypercapnia.

22. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. B — CPAP.
  2. A — Polysomnography.
  3. B — Moderate OSA.
  4. B — Obstructive sleep apnea.
  5. B — CPAP.
  6. B — Bus driver risk.
  7. B — Systemic hypertension.
  8. B — Beyond OSA → OHS.
  9. B — Recurrent upper-airway collapse.
  10. B — Polysomnography.

SBAs

  1. B — Weight loss + CPAP.
  2. B — Sleep study.
  3. B — CPAP.
  4. B — Septoplasty/deviated septum.
  5. B — Weight loss + CPAP.
  6. B — Lifestyle + CPAP.
  7. B — CPAP.
  8. B — 2% nocturnal oxygen.
  9. B — Mandibular advancement.
  10. B — Driving risk.

topics.title: Upper Airway Obstruction And Stridor
topics.html:
[!important]
Stridor is a medical emergency — indicates ≥50% airway narrowing. Causes: anaphylaxis, foreign body, laryngeal oedema, tumour, croup, vocal cord dysfunction. Inspiratory stridor = extrathoracic (laryngeal). Treatment depends on cause — IM adrenaline for anaphylaxis, bronchoscopy for foreign body, intubation/tracheostomy for severe.

Related: [[Asthma]], [[Foreign body aspiration in adults]], [[Vocal cord dysfunction and inducible laryngeal obstruction|VCD]], [[Wheeze and stridor approach]]

[!tip] FCPS/MRCP pearl: Stridor = upper airway emergency. Inspiratory = extrathoracic (laryngeal). Expiratory = intrathoracic (trachea). Biphasic = fixed (subglottic stenosis, tumour). Always exclude anaphylaxis (IM adrenaline), foreign body (bronchoscopy), tumour (laryngoscopy + CT), croup (dexamethasone).

1. Definition

Stridor = monophonic harsh sound from upper airway obstruction (larynx, trachea).

Types

  • Inspiratory stridor: extrathoracic (laryngeal, subglottic) — narrows on inspiration
  • Expiratory stridor: intrathoracic (tracheal) — narrows on expiration
  • Biphasic stridor: fixed obstruction (subglottic stenosis, tracheal tumour)

2. Aetiology

Acute

CauseNotes
AnaphylaxisAcute, with urticaria, hypotension
Foreign bodySudden, often children, unilateral wheeze
Angioedema (hereditary, ACE-i)Facial swelling
CroupChildren 6 months–3 years, barking cough, viral
EpiglottitisChildren, toxic, drooling, sitting up
LaryngospasmPost-extubation, post-thyroidectomy
Bacterial tracheitisSevere, post-viral
Retropharyngeal abscessChildren, fever, drooling
DiphtheriaUnvaccinated, membrane

Subacute / chronic

CauseNotes
Laryngeal tumourSmoker, progressive hoarseness, weight loss
Bilateral vocal cord paralysisPost-thyroidectomy
Subglottic stenosisPost-intubation/tracheostomy
Tracheal stenosisPost-tracheostomy, tumour
Laryngeal papillomatosisChildren, HPV
TracheomalaciaFloppy trachea, dynamic
GoitreCompression
TBMMediastinal mass
LymphomaMediastinal nodes
VCDFunctional, mimics asthma

3. Pathophysiology

Mechanism

  • Air flow through narrowed airway → turbulence → stridor
  • Critical (≥50% narrowing)
  • Inspiratory stridor: extrathoracic (negative intraluminal pressure during inspiration narrows extrathoracic airway)
  • Expiratory stridor: intrathoracic (positive intrathoracic pressure compresses intrathoracic airway)
  • Biphasic: fixed (cartilage)

Acute severe obstruction

  • Complete obstruction → stridor → aphonia → respiratory arrest
  • Time-critical (minutes to hours)

4. Clinical Features

Symptoms

  • Noisy breathing (stridor, wheeze)
  • Dyspnoea
  • Hoarseness (laryngeal)
  • Cough (croupy, barking)
  • Drooling, dysphagia (epiglottitis, abscess)
  • Anaphylaxis symptoms (urticaria, angioedema)

Signs

  • Stridor (auscultate over neck/chest)
  • Drooling, tripod position (epiglottitis)
  • Hoarseness
  • Use of accessory muscles
  • Tracheal tug, recession
  • Cyanosis (severe)
  • Tachycardia, tachypnoea

5. Diagnosis

Investigations

TestPurpose
ABGHypoxaemia, hypercapnia (severe)
CXR (inspiration + expiration)Foreign body, mass, mediastinal
Neck X-ray (AP)Subglottic narrowing (steeple sign in croup)
CT neck/chestMass, abscess, foreign body
BronchoscopyForeign body, tumour, biopsy
LaryngoscopyEpiglottitis, vocal cords
Spirometry (flow-volume loop)Extrathoracic vs intrathoracic obstruction
Allergy testsAnaphylaxis triggers

Flow-volume loop

  • Variable extrathoracic: flattening of inspiratory limb
  • Variable intrathoracic: flattening of expiratory limb
  • Fixed: flattening of both limbs

6. Management

General

  • A–B–C assessment
  • Position: sitting up (avoid supine if epiglottitis)
  • O₂ (humidified)
  • Avoid distressing the patient
  • Senior/anaesthesia/ENT early
  • Keep child with parent

Specific

CauseRx
AnaphylaxisIM adrenaline 0.5 mg (1:1000) + IV fluids + antihistamine + steroids + intubate if needed
CroupDexamethasone (oral) + nebulised adrenaline if severe
EpiglottitisAnaesthesia + intubation (in OR) + ceftriaxone + dexamethasone
Foreign bodyBronchoscopic removal (rigid preferred in children)
Laryngeal oedemaAdrenaline (nebulised), steroids, intubate
Laryngeal tumourENT evaluation, biopsy, oncology
Bilateral VC paralysisTracheostomy
Subglottic stenosisDilatation, stenting
VCDSpeech therapy, reassurance

Don'ts

  • Don't examine throat (epiglottitis) — may cause complete obstruction
  • Don't lie child down (epiglottitis)
  • Don't delay intubation in impending obstruction

7. FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

DomainKey points
Stridor≥50% airway narrowing; emergency
InspiratoryExtrathoracic (laryngeal)
ExpiratoryIntrathoracic (tracheal)
BiphasicFixed (subglottic stenosis, tumour)
AnaphylaxisIM adrenaline
CroupDexamethasone
EpiglottitisAnaesthesia + intubation + abx
Foreign bodyBronchoscopic removal
Don't examine throat in epiglottitis
Laryngeal tumourSmoker, hoarseness

8. MCQs (10)

  1. Inspiratory stridor indicates:

A. Extrathoracic (laryngeal) obstruction

B. Intrathoracic

C. Lower airway

D. None

E. Both

Answer: A — Extrathoracic.

  1. Expiratory stridor indicates:

A. Extrathoracic

B. Intrathoracic (tracheal)

C. Lower airway

D. None

E. Both

Answer: B — Intrathoracic.

  1. Biphasic stridor suggests:

A. Asthma

B. Fixed (subglottic stenosis, tracheal tumour)

C. COPD

D. None

E. Pneumonia

Answer: B — Fixed.

  1. Anaphylaxis with stridor. First:

A. Steroids

B. IM adrenaline 0.5 mg (1:1000)

C. Antihistamine

D. Wait

E. None

Answer: B — IM adrenaline.

  1. The "steeple sign" on neck X-ray suggests:

A. Croup

B. Epiglottitis

C. Foreign body

D. None

E. Asthma

Answer: A — Croup.

  1. The "thumb sign" on lateral neck X-ray suggests:

A. Croup

B. Epiglottitis (swollen epiglottis)

C. Foreign body

D. None

E. Asthma

Answer: B — Epiglottitis.

  1. The most common cause of stridor in adults:

A. Croup

B. Laryngeal tumour (smoker) or laryngeal oedema

C. Epiglottitis

D. None

E. Foreign body

Answer: B — Adult.

  1. Foreign body in airway. Best:

A. Antibiotics

B. Bronchoscopy (rigid preferred)

C. Steroids

D. Wait

E. None

Answer: B — Bronchoscopy.

  1. A 3-year-old with barking cough and stridor. Most likely:

A. Croup (viral)

B. Epiglottitis

C. Asthma

D. Foreign body

E. None

Answer: A — Croup.

  1. The most common cause of post-extubation stridor:

A. Croup

B. Laryngeal oedema

C. Anaphylaxis

D. None

E. Foreign body

Answer: B — Laryngeal oedema.

9. SBA Questions (10)

  1. The most useful investigation for chronic stridor is:

A. Laryngoscopy +/− bronchoscopy + CT

B. CXR

C. Spirogram

D. ABG

E. None

Answer: A — Direct visualisation.

  1. A 60-year-old smoker with hoarseness and stridor. Best:

A. Antibiotics

B. Laryngoscopy + CT (laryngeal cancer workup)

C. Wait

D. Steroids

E. None

Answer: B — Laryngeal cancer.

  1. Acute epiglottitis is treated with:

A. Steroids alone

B. Anaesthesia + intubation + ceftriaxone

C. Antibiotics alone

D. Wait

E. None

Answer: B — Airway + abx.

  1. The most common cause of stridor in a child is:

A. Croup

B. Epiglottitis

C. Foreign body

D. None

E. Tumour

Answer: A — Croup.

  1. The first-line treatment for severe croup:

A. Antibiotics

B. Dexamethasone (oral) + nebulised adrenaline if severe

C. Theophylline

D. Wait

E. None

Answer: B — Dexamethasone ± adrenaline.

  1. Don'ts in suspected epiglottitis:

A. Steroids

B. Examine throat (may cause complete obstruction)

C. Antibiotics

D. None

E. Wait

Answer: B — Don't examine throat.

  1. The most useful imaging for foreign body:

A. CXR (inspiration + expiration) ± CT

B. ABG

C. Spirogram

D. None

E. Echo

Answer: A — CXR ± CT.

  1. The flow-volume loop in fixed obstruction shows:

A. Flattening of both inspiratory and expiratory limbs

B. Normal

C. Restrictive

D. None

E. Variable

Answer: A — Both.

  1. Anaphylaxis with airway oedema requires:

A. Wait

B. IM adrenaline + intubation (if needed)

C. Steroids

D. Antibiotics

E. None

Answer: B — Adrenaline + airway.

  1. A 50-year-old with bilateral vocal cord paralysis causes:

A. Hoarseness

B. Stridor + respiratory failure

C. None

D. Asthma

E. Wheeze

Answer: B — Stridor + RF.

10. Flashcards

  • Q: Inspiratory stridor?

A: Extrathoracic (laryngeal).

  • Q: Expiratory stridor?

A: Intrathoracic (trachea).

  • Q: Biphasic?

A: Fixed.

  • Q: Anaphylaxis?

A: IM adrenaline.

  • Q: Croup?

A: Dexamethasone.

  • Q: Epiglottitis?

A: Anaesthesia + intubation + ceftriaxone.

  • Q: Foreign body?

A: Bronchoscopy (rigid).

  • Q: Laryngeal tumour?

A: Smoker, hoarseness.

  • Q: Steeple sign?

A: Croup.

  • Q: Thumb sign?

A: Epiglottitis.

11. Answer Key with Explanations

MCQs

  1. A 2. B 3. B 4. B 5. A 6. B 7. B 8. B 9. A 10. B

SBAs

  1. A 2. B 3. B 4. A 5. B 6. B 7. A 8. A 9. B 10. B

12. Summary

Stridor = ≥50% airway narrowing; emergency. Inspiratory = extrathoracic (laryngeal); expiratory = intrathoracic (trachea); biphasic = fixed. Anaphylaxis: IM adrenaline. Croup: dexamethasone. Epiglottitis: anaesthesia + intubation + ceftriaxone. Foreign body: bronchoscopy. Don't examine throat in epiglottitis.
